- DGCA CPL Air Regulations Syllabus Explained | Full Breakdown + Study Plan: Kind: captions Language: en Want to master irregulations for your CPL exams without wasting time or money? In this video, I'll break down the entire irregulation syllabus into detailed, easy to understand modules. We'll cover each topic you need to know for the exam and how to remember it. So whether you are a student pilot or just getting started, this is your ultimate guide. Let's dive in. And don't forget if you want structured guidance, mock tests and personal mentorship, check out my DVCA ground classes link in the description below. So the first module definition and abbreviation. This is your foundation. Here you'll learn standard definitions used in the aviation like what airspace means, the definition of flight visibility and abbreviations such as ATC, FIR, VFR, IFR and more. These aren't just for theory. You will learn them in real life operation. Many MCQs are directly picked from this sections. So learn and run. Next and very important module of regulations which is rules of air. This section covers all the rules every pilot must follow while flying from collision avoidance, rightway rules to light signals and formation flying. You will also cover VMC and IMC conditions. Questions often test what a pilot should do in specific scenarios. Master the logic behind each rule. Don't just memorize. Air traffic services also known as ATS. You will learn how the entire ATC system works. ATS includes flight information services, alerting services, air traffic advisory services. This helps you understand how aircrafts are managed in controlled and uncontrolled airspace. Know the difference between procedural and radar based separations. Area and approach control services. This digs deeper into how controllers assist aircraft in end route and terminal phases. It explains transfer of control, communication and overs and separation standards. This section directly ties to real life flying and practical ATC interactions. The next module is aerodyome control services. Everything that happens on ground at an airport from taxi instructions to runway clearances and big turbulence separations. These rules apply from engine start to takeoff and after landing. The next module is aeronautical information services AIS. Here you learn about NOTAMS, AIPs and how critical information is distributed to pilots. AIS ensures safe and efficient flight planning. It also includes the structure and content of aeronautical publications. Visuals for navigation. These are lights, markings, and sign boards you see on runways and taxiway. Learn about puppy lights, runway edge lights, stop bars, holding position signs and more. This helps you both in exam and real world flying. Panops standard of procedures for air navigation services, aircraft operations. It deals with instrument approach procedures, obstacle clearance limits and how these procedures are designed. You don't need to design charts but you must understand the protections areas and terminology used like prohibited areas, restricted areas and danger areas. Next comes personal licensing. This section includes requirements for CPL, PPL, ATPL licenses, medicals and type rating and instrument ratings. Know the documents required privileges for each license renewals and recency requirements. Operational procedures. What a pilot should do in specific operations like fuel dumping, arnav approaches, ETOPS, RBSM operations, engine out procedures. Questions here are logic based often using real world airlines SOP types scenarios. You'll also learn about flight duty, rest period, and duty limitations on flight crew for domestic and international operations. Special operational procedures and hazards for low visibility takeoffs to volcanic ash avoidance, bird strike procedures, emergency landing protocols and operations in contaminated runways, dangerous boots and laws guarding them. This section is vital. Expect scenario based questions. Next, communications covers radio telephoneony procedures, distress and emergency calls, readback requirement and standard phrasiology. Learn correct call sign usage, lost communication procedures and standard emergency phrases. This is also important for your RTR human performance. This is one of the most important topics in air regulations. This module covers circulatory and respiratory systems, hypoxia, hyperventilation and time of useful consciousness, the nervous system and sensory illusions, vision and earing issues, gforces and how human factors contributes to day-to-day flying and its limitations. Expect physiology based MCQs and focus on aviation relevance. Next, aircraft nationality and registration, accident and facilitation. Covers rules of how aircraft are registered and identified globally, search and rescue responsibilities and post accident protocols. Also includes rules for customs, immigration and handling of passengers, airworthiness and aviation security. Learn what makes an aircraft legally airworthy. Understand certificates of airworthiness maintenance requirements and anti-ijacking security rules under ICA NX7. National law and Indian civil aviation regulations. This is India specific. Learn about DGCA and AI airport authority of India rules. Understand Indian cars which is civil aviation requirements. The aircraft act 1934 and rules 1937. You will get three to four MCQs directly from this question. International organization and conventions covers ICAV, IATA, Chicago conventions and Tokyo, Montreal and O conventions. This regulate everything from international flights to hijacking laws. Next, ATS surveillance systems includes radar systems, ADSB, and how ATC monitors aircraft. Learn about primary and secondary transponder codes and their meanings like 750, 760, 7700. So that was a complete breakdown of DGCA CPL air regulation syllabus. Study smart. Focus on scenario- based understanding, not just memorization. Attempt quizzes weekly and revise tricky sections. If you want a structured course with the daily plans, mock quizzes, live sessions, and a personal guidance, join my DGCA ground classes link in the description below. And for free quizzes and resources to test yourself right now, check out the pin comment below. I am Sudashan from Fly with Sudashan, helping you clear DGCA exams and chase the skies. Subscribe if you are serious about becoming a commercial pilot.

personnel and flight operations officers this Annex ensures that all Aviation personnel meet the stringent criteria set by the international civil aviation organization or Ico providing a uniform level of Competency Worldwide Moving On to Annex 2 rules of the air this Annex guides the conduct of flights internationally it sets out the general rules and procedures for air navigation ensuring that all aircraft are operated in a manner that promotes safety and efficiency it covers everything from visual flight rules and instrument flight rules to air traffic control procedures and emergency protocols this Annex essentially serves as the rulebook for the skies facilitating the smooth flow of air traffic globally lastly we have Annex 3 meteorological services for International Air that accurate and timely weather information is available to support safe and efficient flight operations it covers areas such as weather forecasting the provision of meteorological data and the use of meteorological information in air traffic management as we can see these annexes lay the groundwork for ensuring safety and efficiency in aviation moving on to the next set of annexes we have Annex 4 aeronautical charts Annex 5 units of measurement and Annex 6 operation of aircraft each of these plays a crucial role in the world of Aviation providing pilots and Aviation professionals with essential tools guidelines and standards first up Annex 4 or aeronautical charts a pilot's best friend these charts are like road maps for the sky guiding Pilots through the vast expanse of airspace they come in various forms each designed for a specific purpose there are on- route charts for navigating during flights approach charts for landing and area charts for complex airspace around major airports they detail everything from topography and airspace classes to radio frequencies all colorcoded for easy interpretation next we have Annex 5 the units of measurement this Annex standardizes the units used in international Aviation it's the reason why no matter where you are in the world altitude is measured in feet speed in knots and distance in nautical miles this uniformity is key ensuring clear and consistent community Comm unication between all parties involved in a flight's operation finally we delve into Annex 6 operation of aircraft this Annex lays down the law for aircraft operations outlining everything from pre-flight procedures and in-flight operations to postflight checks it covers operational safety measures performance operating limitations and even the documentation required on board an aircraft Annex 6 is like a comprehensive manual ensuring that every flight operated meets the highest standards of safety and efficiency in essence these annexes provide the necessary tools and guidelines to operate aircraft safely and efficiently they form the backbone of Aviation operations ensuring that every flight whether it's a short hop or a long haul journey is carried out with utmost precision and safety next up are annexes 7 to5 covering a broad range of topics from aircraft nationality and registration marks to aeronautical Information Services starting with Annex 7 we delve into the world of aircraft nationality and registration marks this Annex establishes the principles for nationality marks such as the N for the United States or the G for the United Kingdom and registration marks that uniquely identify each aircraft next we have Annex 8 which involves airworthiness of aircraft this Annex outlines the standards for the design manufacturer and maintenance of aircraft to ensure they're safe to fly an 9 is all about the facilitation of International Air transport ensuring that the processes at airports run smoothly and efficiently it covers everything from Immigration and Customs procedures to health and quarantine measures moving on to Annex 10 this one is all about aeronautical telecommunications it's split into six volumes covering various aspects of communication from voice communication systems to surveillance and collision avoidance systems now let's dive into the world of air traff traffic services with Annex 11 this Annex lays out the standards and methods for air traffic Services aiming to prevent collisions between aircraft both on the ground and in the air next we have Annex 12 which covers search and rescue operations it outlines the international standards for the planning coordination and conduct of search and rescue operations to ensure the Swift recovery of those in distress Annex 13 is a significant one as it deals with aircraft accident and incident investigation it provides guidelines to ensure that investigations are conducted thoroughly and impartially to prevent future accidents the next two annexes 14 and 15 deal with aerodromes and aeronautical Information Services respectively Annex 14 provides specifications for Designing and operating aerodromes while Annex 15 ensures the timely availability of essential aeronautical information these annexes are integral to the smooth running of air traffic services and the provision of essential information to Aviation Personnel lastly we have annexes 16 to19 which deal with Environmental Protection emergency procedures and Safety Management these annexes are integral to the functioning and sustainability of the global aviation industry Annex 16 is all about Environmental Protection it sets out the standards and recommended practices for aircraft noise and engine emissions these guidelines help to minimize the impact of Aviation on the environment and contribute to the development of Greener more sustainable Aviation Technologies moving on Annex 17 is dedicated to Security safeguarding International civil aviation against acts of unlawful interference it's all about ensuring the Safety and Security of passengers crew ground personnel and the general public next we have Annex 18 which deals with the Safe Transportation of dangerous goods by air this Annex is crucial because it provides the necessary guidelines for ensuring that hazardous materials are transported safely and efficiently without posing a risk to people or the environment finally Annex 19 focuses on Safety Management this anex introduces a proactive approach to managing safety requiring Airlines and Aviation service providers to implement a safety management system it's all about identifying assessing and mitigating risks before they can escalate into bigger issues these final annexes underscore io's commitment to environmental sustainability and safety and Aviation they highlight the importance of mitigating the environmental impact of Aviation ensuring the security of all Aviation activities safely transporting dangerous goods and proactively managing safety risks these annexes demonstrate that in the world of Aviation safety and sustainability are not just aspirations but fundamental requirements so why are these IO annexes so important well these 19 annexes are the backbone of international Aviation a global rule book if you will they set Universal standards and recommended practices to ensure safety regularity and efficiency in International Air transport from Personnel licensing to meteorological services from aircraft operations to Safety Management these annexes cover all aspects of Aviation they also play a crucial role in promoting sustainable practices in aviation helping us all fly into a Greener future that wraps up our Deep dive into the icio annexes remember these regulations make it possible for us to fly safely and efficiently across the globe

- The Race To Stay Profitable In The Air Industry | CNBC Marathon: Kind: captions Language: en The airline industry is a long-cycle business. Spirit has had one challenge after another coming out of the pandemic. The icon of budget air travel in the U.S. has not been profitable since 2019, and it lost over $2 billion since 2020. Airlines that have shown more success have greater exposure to premium and corporate. The biggest competitor that United has right now is Delta. United very much wants to rule the world. But when it comes to profitability, it's still chasing that number one spot. Everybody's dealing with the fact that costs are 30% higher than they were in 2019. Some airlines decided to place orders with Airbus for aircraft rather than Boeing. ...Which has been dealing with crisis after crisis. Airbus has profited from the challenges that Boeing has faced. We're really talking about a battle for the 2030s here. The 2020s have been decided. Airbus and Boeing have been competing head-to-head for decades as the world's largest commercial airplane makers. The European-based Airbus entered the market 56 years after its rival. And today, 76% of the world's fleet is made up of Airbus and Boeing planes. But over the last few years, Airbus has edged out Boeing for market dominance. Airbus is clearly in the lead and they have a good lead. And if you just want to measure that, it's by backlog of the airplanes. Airbus has made a steady profit since the pandemic. While Boeing hasn't made a profit since 2018. Airbus total backlog, which are planes that have been ordered but not yet delivered, is 8,726, compared to Boeing's 5,643, which represents billions of dollars in revenue and is estimated to take almost 13 years to complete. We've gone from back in 1978 where you would hardly ever see an Airbus airplane in the U.S., to now having well over 50% of the market here in the U.S. Since 2019, Airbus has delivered more planes annually than Boeing, which has been dealing with crisis after crisis, including two deadly crashes of its best-selling 737 Max eight, which grounded that aircraft for nearly two years. It's also been dealing with significant financial losses, manufacturing issues and production limitations. One thing I want to be clear about: Airbus was already winning the race before the Max ran into trouble because the A321 in general was winning share. Narrowbodies make up the majority of the commercial aircraft flying today. These planes are very versatile. They can do multiple short trips a day or across oceans and continents. Boeing 737 was the world's best-selling narrow-body until 2019, when Airbus A320 surpassed it in total orders. And depending on how many planes are delivered in 2025, the A320 could take the reins as the most produced jet family in history. If you look across the board at the backlogs and indeed at orders, everything is fine in every category except for the A321, which is absolutely crushing it. CNBC explores how Airbus came to be the biggest commercial plane manufacturer and whether it can stay on top. The official 737 christening ceremony took place in the new final assembly building. In the late '60s, Boeing was dominating the commercial aircraft market. It had just debuted the 737 and the world's first ever wide-body jet. The massive 747 European aerospace companies took notice. The biggest misconception about Airbus is that it was some sort of instant creation, circa 1969. It really wasn't. It was a merger of a number of European national champion aerospace companies that had been building planes since World War Two or World War One, and the only way they could do it was by pooling their resources: thus Airbus. As more people took to the skies, airlines looked to operate bigger aircraft. At the time, all wide-body planes had 3 or 4 engines. For reference, a wide-body typically has two aisles, flies longer distances, and can carry anywhere from 250 to 500 passengers. While a narrow-body or single aisle has one single aisle through, the cabin flies shorter distances and can have 100 to 230 seats. Airbus shows up in the 1970s saying, "Hey, we have a wide-body plane, but with just two engines." And airlines sat up and said, "Whoa, two engines..." And obviously two engines that can take you the same distance as three are better because your maintenance costs are a lot lower. In 1972, Airbus introduced the first twin-engine wide-body jetliner: the A300. Despite having only two engines, the A300 could only carry around 250 passengers versus the massive 747, which could be configured to fit over 500. Not a lot of people saw the need for an intermediate sized yet. So, production rates were 1 or 2 per month for quite a few years. In 1978, the Deregulation Act was enacted in the U.S., creating more competition, new airlines and expansion of smaller ones. As airline needs shifted in the '80s, the demand for cost-effective, short-haul aircraft grew. They began to see the need for a new-generation single aisle, basically centered around 150 seats. Airbus had not yet made a profit, and the company needed a plane in its fleet to compete with Boeing's popular narrow-body, the 737, which had already been around since 1967. Airbus launched the A320 single aisle program in 1984. The workhorse of the Airbus commercial jet family is the A320. The A320 was one of the few commercial aircraft designed to compete against Boeing's 737 powerhouse, and has succeeded where others have failed. Its fuselage is a little bit wider than the 737, and some airlines have taken advantage of that to put in slightly wider seats. The A320 debuted with a lot of new advanced technology. It was one of the first commercial jets that used a side stick versus yoke controls. This is still a key difference between Airbus and Boeing planes today. It also introduced fly-by-wire controls, meaning when you pull back on the side stick, it sends electronic signals to a computer to move the aircraft versus a physical cable. One could argue from a flight control's point of view, the A320 is a generation ahead of the 737. When it first came into the market, it was controversial. Fly-by-wire when it first came out into the market was something that was really just used in military airplanes. The jet's debut, however, ended in a crash during the first demonstration flight at an airshow in 1988. When the A320 was first introduced, it suffered a series of accidents, including some fatal accidents that killed crew and passengers, and that really stalled the public acceptance of the plane. It took a while there, too, to get traction, but when it did, well, it succeeded far beyond expectations. A320 just came into the world 20 years later and has that advantage in terms of just fundamental design differences to the airframe. By the 1990s, the A320 success would start to put pressure on its U.S. competitors. Airbus, which was backed by European governments, made its first operating profits. Once Airbus was more market-proven as an aircraft builder and U.S. airlines started to see the performance results of the Airbus aircraft in other airlines' fleets, they became more open to buying Airbus planes, and it was really the A320 that helped Airbus crack the door opened into the U.S. market and many other parts of the world. Basically, Boeing through all of this had 50% of the market. So, what you basically had was Douglas, Lockheed and Airbus duking it out for the other half. Lockheed exited sometime in the mid-80s, and then everyone knew McDonnell Douglas. Matter of time. In 1997, McDonnell Douglas merged with Boeing, forming the duopoly we know today. If you think about the evolution of Airbus, I mean, Airbus really started in the narrow-body market with no market share, edged out Douglas, and then continued to get share. The Boeing 737 family and the Airbus A320 family are the company's best-selling planes, and have been a crucial segment of competition. The list price for one A321neo is around $129 million. Competition was head-to-head when it came to wide-bodies too. In the '90s, more fuel-efficient, twin-engine wide-bodies were introduced. Airbus debuted the A330 and 340, while Boeing introduced its 777. The A330, they kept refining it at Airbus. It did pretty well, and then Boeing attacked with the 787 Dreamliner. And despite all of the flaws baked into that development program, it did better than the A330 in the long run. Then, from 2003 to 2005, Airbus took more orders and delivered more planes than Boeing. That 50-50 formula kept persisting, and what Boeing was doing was effectively winning on profitability rather than market share. They were willing to say, "Alright, we're going to be 50%, but our margins are going to be 9% and yours are going to be 4%...or less." It was a formula that worked for them until, of course, the problems with the Max. Despite declining sales for four engine jets, Airbus spent billions creating the biggest double-decker quad-engine commercial aircraft in the world and possibly the most identifiable Airbus plane: the A380. It was in service from 2007 until it ended production in 2021. The A380 is an engineering marvel. Unfortunately, in a way, the A380's success was eclipsed by the success of Airbus's smaller aircraft, namely the A330 family and more recently, the A350, which are twin engine aircraft that can hold 250 to 400, 450 or so passengers and operate ultra-long range routes. And you looked at Airbus and you thought, "Wow, they're going to be tied for number one. You might even be number one. The only thing you could do to screw that up is launch a superjumbo for a market that's going away super fast." And that's exactly what they did. It cost them a decade. Today, Boeing still dominates the wide-body market with nearly 56% of the global fleet. These aircraft are used for long haul travel like international flights. Boeing currently has a backlog of over 1,500 wide-body planes. The company has had multiple manufacturing issues with its 787 Dreamliner and the 777X, which has yet to be certified, is years behind schedule. In 2012, Airbus announced it would begin manufacturing in Mobile, Alabama. You go back to 1978 and Airbus had a small office here in the U.S. with about a dozen people working there. Roll forward to 2025, and we have a very large manufacturing facility that manufactures airplanes in Mobile, Alabama. We build helicopters in Mississippi, we build satellites in Florida and actually are present in over 40 locations across the U.S. today. Airbus' biggest customer is Delta Airlines. It has slightly more Airbus planes in its fleet than Boeing. Airbus has had a different manufacturing strategy than Boeing. Theirs has been more regional, so they have a final assembly plant, a fowl, as they call them, in Asia, one in North America, one in Europe. Where Boeing's final assembly has been highly concentrated in North America, China was almost exclusively a Boeing customer for many, many years. By having a final assembly line in China, I think Airbus would argue that that helped them get some share in China. Airbus has been manufacturing in the U.S. for nearly ten years. It's about to open a third production line, two of which are for the 320 family and the other for its smaller 220 jets it acquired from Bombardier in In 2010, Airbus announced a new variation of its best-selling narrow-body called the A320neo, short for "new engine option," which promised a 15% less fuel burn than the previous generation. In 2011, Airbus had a record number of orders for 1,419 aircraft, more than double than the year before. That same year, American Airlines, which had been strictly a Boeing customer, put in an order for hundreds of these new planes. And in 2019, the A320 family surpassed the 737 in total orders. Originally, the business case was for around 600 airplanes, and now we are up to 19,000. Boeing's response to the more fuel-efficient A320 was the re-engined 737 Max that debuted in 2017. But over the next few years, the manufacturer faced a series of challenges. In 2018 and 2019, two 737 Max 8s crashed and killed 346 people. It was later found a flight control system known as MCAS had malfunctioned on both flights. The planes were grounded for several months and production rates were reduced. Then the pandemic brought air travel to a halt, and the company had a record loss of $12 billion in 2020. Airbus saw losses of over 1 billion in both 2019 and 2020, but returned to profitability in 2021, but Airbus has consecutively had more annual orders and deliveries since 2019, making it the world's largest planemaker. Then, in 2024, a door plug flew off a Boeing 737 Max 9 plane, leading to more production delays of its best-selling jet and an annual loss of $11.8 billion in 2024. Some airlines decided to place orders with Airbus for aircraft rather than Boeing. And if you look at Airbus' backlog today, it's dominated by A321. Airlines looking to cut down on fuel costs have been turning to these more efficient, longer range narrow-bodies for longer flights like JFK to LA or the East Coast to Europe. Of our single aisle order book or backlog as we call it, 72% of the Airbus A321, and that's opening up routes and markets that they couldn't fly directly before. The economics with single aisles were so much better than the economics with wide-bodies. And the Airbus A321neo wasn't perfect, but it was the biggest and best single aisle you could get, and leading Airbus to effectively a 60% or 65% market share from the standpoint of both the deliveries and orders and backlog. It's become the hinge factor between the two. Boeing doesn't really have a comparable airplane. 737 Max 9 isn't quite as big. The 10 is big, but it doesn't quite have the range. So, where Airbus has done well in the market is they have a product that Boeing really doesn't have. The 737 Max 10 has a range of 3,100 nautical miles and can seat a maximum of 230 people, versus the A321neo, which has a range of 4,700 nautical miles and can seat 244 passengers. But the Max 10 has yet to be certified. The bad news is, given these delays, these many year delays, and given the challenges, and also, frankly, you've got this attack on the administrative state in Washington, which is also toxic from Boeing's standpoint, these are resources they need to certify planes, and they're increasingly in short supply. So, there's a lot about DOGE and the attack on the FAA and other administrative agencies that's really not all good from the standpoint of Boeing's certification. In 2024, Airbus delivered 766 planes compared to Boeing's 348. Most of the revenue from aircraft is received on delivery. Boeing's 737 Max production has been capped at 38 planes a month since January 2024 by the Federal Aviation Administration. That rate was 52 planes a month prior to the pandemic and two Max crashes. So far in 2025, it surpassed monthly delivery goals compared to last year. Airbus has faced production challenges too. It, like the rest of the industry, has been dealing with supply chain issues post-pandemic and an engine recall for many of its jets. There was the hot GTF engine two years ago, with the difficulties they encountered with the metal powder issue that is leading to a big recall campaign, and that is limiting the number of engines going for new feed for us. Also last year, with production difficulties faced by CFM and their supply chain, that has led to a difficult end of 2024 and also some shortages at the beginning of this year. Airbus deliveries have been lower compared to last year, but the company said it still expects to deliver 820 new planes by year-end. The medium term, it's hard to see anything that stops Airbus from controlling that all-important mid-market segment. The 2020s have been decided. The industry could face other challenges, like tariffs and a possible recession in the coming months. For an industry that's thoroughly globalized, this is absolutely toxic. There's a lot about these tariffs we just don't know right now. But, it's remarkable, the extent to which the jetliner industry became a model of free trade and the U.S. competed and absolutely did great with an enormous trade surplus: and that's now at risk. Now, as for the airplanes themselves, yeah, the majority of structures are indeed imported. Even though Airbus now has manufacturing in Mobile, Alabama, it still imports parts like wings and fuselages. The U.S. exports six times more than imports in aerospace products and components. And so, the concern about a tariff regime is that that is then going to impede in that supply chain, and it's going to add to the cost of the airplane. More expensive airplanes could mean rising costs for the consumer. Both the Boeing 737 and the Airbus A320, and their variations are newer interpretations of jets designed decades ago. The industry has been waiting for new technologies, engines and designs to be introduced. The A320 has been a platform that's been around for many decades. The next generation airplane will be a completely new platform. It's going to be based on new propulsion techniques and new wing design and new materials, and also a more digitally enabled airplane. We hope to see that flying in the second half of the next decade. It is so much more complex to design and build a new airliner today than it was decades ago. Airlines are struggling with what is the right design, what materials go into it, what engines do we use for it, what fuel powers it? In 2022, Boeing's CEO at the time, Dave Calhoun, announced that it would not introduce a new airplane into the next decade. So, on that range and payload envelope, they've really got nothing that competes with the 321neo. They need a new jet. They absolutely need a new jet. So that, of course, told people that they should go and order an A321neo, which they did. There were 1,300 sales in the following 12 months. So, Dave Calhoun, in a lot of ways, was the very best CEO that Airbus ever had. Airbus hasn't announced a new clean-sheet plane either, but at a recent summit, outlined what technologies could potentially power a new single aisle aircraft. Airbus is just positioned better because they just have more latitude to kind of wait and see because of their product lineup today. CNBC explores how United became one of the largest airlines in the world, and what's next for the carrier. United Airlines' roots date back all the way to 1926. The original name of United was Boeing Air Transport, and it was owned by the Boeing Company until Boeing had to spin it off in the late '20s, early '30s when it became United. It began by delivering mail and continued to grow as the dawn of the jet age took more people to the skies. United Airlines has a very, very strong and rich history, but the United Airlines we know today is actually the product of various mergers. All the top airlines have grown through mergers. United's first big merger was in 1961, with Capital Airlines, expanding its reach to more of the East Coast. In 1985, they bought Pan Am's Transpacific route network, which gave them an expansive route network into Asia. Another was announced in 2010 when it agreed to merge with Continental Airlines. Think of United as a Brady Bunch of airlines that now has a single name and a single paint job. The Continental merger was really significant. What it did is it really set up the modern day United Airlines. Over the course of a century, the airline has faced many challenges. On September 11th, 2001, two of the planes hijacked by terrorists were United planes. Then air travel demand plummeted. The United States was also in the middle of a recession. So, what we saw was several airlines, U.S. Airways then United Airlines had filed for bankruptcy in the years following the terrorist attacks. It came out of bankruptcy in 2006 with a reduced staff and fleet. The Great Recession in 2008, coupled with rising fuel costs, hit the industry hard. Then there was a series of mega mergers Delta and Northwest American and U.S. Airways and United with Continental. The difficulties in that merger were many and varied. Trying to put two large corporations together is always difficult. You had two very different cultures. Oscar Munoz was on the board of United Continental Holdings in 2010, and later served as the CEO of United from 2015 to 2020. There was not a lot of love between the two organizations and groups. The company was not in a wonderful place with regards to unison cohesion. A singular point of attack, operationally, financially, business-wise. So it was a bit of a mish-mash. The $8.5 billion deal with Continental brought key hubs like Newark and Houston into the United network. You had two of everything. So, how do you decide the systems and the people to run them when you have this quote unquote, merger of equals, where it's kind of top-weighted towards one side, most of those systems, most of those leaders came over to run the much, much bigger organization. United Airlines is an airline that is trying to be a more premium airline right now, much as Delta has been for more than ten years. We've been able to offer more seats at more price points, and that has allowed us to compete with the low-cost carriers in a price effective way for the basic economy traveler, but it also allows us to compete with the foreign flag carriers by offering a better product in the front of the cabin. That's also helped create a moat around United Airlines to drive sustainable earnings. In general, across United, we sacrifice seats in the coach cabin to make sure there's more Economy Plus seats in each economy cabin for consumers to pick from. And it saw that fares were going down in some of its key markets. They don't like someone coming in and offering these double-digit fares into their markets. So, what Delta did and sort of kicked off this industry trend was they offered their own sort of barebones fares: it was called "basic economy." Typically, a basic economy fare is very restrictive and only includes the flight. It is nonrefundable, non-changeable. Passengers can't pick their seat and board the plane later. A barebones fare on legacy airlines still comes with nonalcoholic beverages and a snack. On Delta and JetBlue, a basic economy ticket still comes with free Wi-Fi. What a deal to realize is that the base fare was a nice hook for the ULCCs, and by the time the passenger went through the booking process and put everything together, they were now paying a fair that Delta was willing to support. Travelers preferences have changed coming out of the pandemic, with many consumers willing to spend on better seats or perks like lounges. Delta said its premium revenue has outpaced standard seats for several years. United said it's been sacrificing economy seats to add more Economy Plus seats, its extra legroom It reported a 5% rise in premium revenue and 20% rise in basic economy tickets from a year ago. And as United upgauges, I think they're going to have more seats to dedicate to basic economy. But I look at that more as a defensive tool than a ULCC killer. But it does make life harder for ultra low-cost carriers competing in these large markets with the legacy carriers. The pressure on ultra low-cost airlines has led to some big changes. Spirit and Frontier got rid of most ticket change fees last year and started offering pricing bundles. That includes things they used to charge separately for. Also, while Spirit already offers travelers the big front seat, Frontier said it will install first class seats to its fleet. Frontier has also had a period of losing money, but they've had other challenges. Frontier CEO Barry Biffle said one of the biggest gaps in the company's revenue model came from not offering first class seats. Even Southwest Airlines, the fourth biggest carrier in the U.S., is making changes. The airline that has had open seating and one cabin service for over 50 years is now adding extra legroom seats and ending open seating to improve revenue. With Southwest, you're seeing a lot of just evolving with customer demand that maybe is 5 to 10 years too late, but they're making that change. You know, where they fly has changed, how full the planes have become has changed. And so, that brings a change in customer preference of wanting this surety of an assigned seat. As a result, airline cabins have become more segmented to offer different products for multiple price points. Many now offer more than just coach and first class. A long haul flight could have first, business, premium economy, extra legroom, regular and basic economy options. Everybody's kind of recognizing that people are willing to pay up for experiences, and it's interesting where most of the changes are happening is in the middle end of the experience segment. So, it's not first class seats that are getting added. It's really premium economy. That model is so important to airlines' loyalty programs, which is really what drives their profits these days. It's had enormous growth, these credit card agreements with some of the country's biggest banks, bring in billions of dollars for airlines. And if you don't offer products that customers can cash in those points for, then you're toast. Even though Spirit has filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection, that doesn't mean the airline ceases to exist. The airline will continue to operate and sell tickets for future flights. Analysts expect Spirit to come out of this process a smaller airline with fewer routes. One thing that is likely to happen in Spirit's bankruptcy is they need to get their costs in check, and they need to do it very quickly. They've already sold a chunk of their fleet to raise cash. They are also likely to lay off more of their workforce. They furloughed more than 300 but there is probably more shrink in the company to come. Merger talks could also resume in 2025. I think a combined Spirit and Frontier give them the best chance of really giving a good competition to the legacy carriers and the low-cost carriers, because you get a ubiquitous network, you get economies of scale and a lot of benefits that can help you go after that market. Spirit said in an emailed statement to CNBC that its new travel options and increased flexibility reflect our ongoing efforts to provide greater value and comfort, and that it is well positioned for long-term success and are confident that we will become an even stronger competitor upon emergence from the Chapter 11 process. With all of these challenges facing questions remain about whether the low-cost model is dead or if it's just evolving. They are recognizing that they can appeal to a passenger that wants a little bit better experience, like extra legroom, maybe a little bit more service in-flight. So, they are recognizing that and trying to appeal to that and expand their customer base a little bit more. But in general, I don't think the low-cost carrier industry is dead. It just needs to be resized for the current realities.

- Why Airlines Can’t Survive Without Loyalty Programs | WSJ Case Study: Kind: captions Language: en - [Narrator] Fully booked flights, limited bag space, and a kid kicking the window seat you paid an extra $100 for. - I am an extreme occasion FOC, fear of coach. - [Narrator] But that's the reality for the majority of people flying today. A stark shift from the days where air travel included complimentary cocktails and an in-flight piano performance. So, what happened? To understand, we have to go back to a decision made decades ago, when deregulation allowed airlines to set their own flights and routes. What was meant to make the industry more competitive, ultimately set off a race to increase profits and cement an airline's market share, leading to the world's first airline loyalty program. - It's hard to see how airlines could survive today without loyalty programs. - If we just started from scratch without it, I don't know. - [Narrator] Today, airlines make on average only about $13 in profit per passenger. But 2020 revealed that the loyalty program can hold a higher value than the airline itself. Here's the case study on how American Airlines created its own form of currency, changing the way the world flies and creating an entirely new business that has nothing to do with aviation. - This was back in an environment where airlines weren't full. - [Narrator] In the late 1970s, American Airlines was losing some of its grip on the market. - [Hal] De-regulation had meant that there were lots of new airlines. - Suddenly, it's a little bit of a free-for-all. - Everybody was trying to figure out how can we motivate people to fly more. - [Narrator] New, smaller carriers began winning market share by offering lower prices, and the competition forced the traditional carriers to rethink their business model. They wanted to keep flying the same routes, while maintaining prices, but not lose customers to competitors. - So, our advertising agency said to us, why don't you guys see if you can come up with some kind of a prize? - [Narrator] The company brought together a small team to brainstorm. - Bob Crandall, he was the chief executive of American Airlines at the time, and they brought in an outside consultant named Hal Brierley. - I definitely give Bob Crandall credit as the visionary who knew that incentives could drive behavior. - [Narrator] The team was inspired by price stamps, which could be collected at various stores and then exchanged for rewards like a watch or a new camera. And American transitioned that idea to give customers miles, the first iteration of advantage. - I think it did sound like a sort of simple idea at the time that if you give people some kind of prize, they might buy tickets. - [Narrator] In the early days, that simply meant a free flight. American Airlines was betting that the business the program attracted would be greater than the cost to redeem the reward. But to nail that, American needed to set the right benchmark. The company knew that its average consumer flew about 40,000 miles a year. - Setting 50 meant, concentrate your travel, fly on us more. - [Narrator] The team also implemented a specific one-year time limit for the program and developed a way to track their customers. - They knew this was Tom Jones, who flew from Miami to New York. They didn't know if this was the same Tom Jones who flew to Paris last month. - Computerized reservation systems were still new and innovative and just getting off the ground. - [Narrator] To solve this, American linked its reward and reservation system and assigned customers a specific number, inspired by rental car company Avis. - The goal was to know which customers were the most profitable. Obviously, if I knew what you flew last year, I can design incentives that will reward you for incremental spend. - [Narrator] After more than a year of planning, American Airlines lodged advantage on May 1st, 1981. (bright music) - [Alison] It seemed like the reaction was almost immediate. - [Narrator] United matched within a week, and shortly after, TWA, Continental, Northwest Orient, Breneth, and Texas International all followed. - And literally, by September, every airline but one had a program. - [Narrator] But the secret weapon American had over its competitors at the time was just that, time. - They had a one-year lead. - So, the other airlines had not done the planning necessary to keep track of the miles. While they were figuring that, we were signing up their customers. - American thought that we might have 500,000 people. We signed up in the first year, a million people. - [Narrator] But the influx of competitors through a wrench in American's lead. When United launched its program without a one-year time limit on the miles. - I think a lot of the airlines thought it was a one-year promotion. And it might have been a one-year promotion had United not made it open-ended. - [Narrator] American quickly removed its limit as well. - And of course, the consequence of that is that every frequent fire wants to belong to every frequent fire program. And they do. - And that's totally destroyed the initial economics. (light music) - [Narrator] Without the time limit, the airlines found themselves once again one-upping each other. Over and over and over again. - I said we need a gold program. We need something that recognizes the top two percent. - [Narrator] This created a system to determine levels of incentive. The more miles, the more incentives. Like a complimentary first-class upgrade. These upgrades were also a way to accustom flyers to the more expensive accommodations. So that eventually, they would buy that option on their own. - [Bob] Now the advantage from them is hugely profitable because the value of the prize is proportional to the amount of business. - [Narrator] Miles became a sort of currency as frequent flyer programs began attracting attention and capital from outside the aviation industry, specifically from banks. The airlines began selling miles to these banks in exchange for cash. The bank would then offer these miles to their card holders as a reward. But this movement that passengers aren't an airline's only customer. - I mean, for the airline, that's great because it means they get revenue from all sorts of goods and services that you buy that are totally independent from travel or travel demands. - So, the advantage problem has become the business in its own right at a very profitable point. - [Narrator] But by awarding miles for spending, these programs were no longer just about frequent flyers. Instead, it's about money spent. - How much did you spend on your ticket becomes more important than how far you're flying. So, you don't necessarily need to be a road warrior whose boss sends you out on business travel four days a week to have a shot at this. You can get there by spending under credit card. - [Narrator] Prompting the debate. Have airlines become more like banks that just happen to fly? - I think the airlines disagree with that, and the flying is sort of the point. - There is no program without the airline. - But in terms of the role that the programs play in their finances, I mean they have become really prominent, and it's hard to ignore. I mean, today, often in some quarters or some parts of the year, the actual airline operation doesn't make money. You know, costs more to fly passengers around than they pay the airline. What can fill in the gaps is the loyalty program and the co-branded credit card that gives airlines a little bit of revenue every time a customer swipes that credit card. - [Narrator] In 2023, American Airlines brought in $5.2 billion from its credit card and other partnerships. And Delta, $6.6 billion. But the offerings promised in a loyalty program have become more competitive as the audience grows. Now, customers are paying for these accommodations. By 2022, and silary revenues like that made from seat upgrades and baggage fees accounted for about 15% of total airline revenue. - When I started, when you bought a ticket, everything came with it. Whether you consumed it or not. What we've been able to do over time is actually create more products that enable customers to pay for what they're going to consume. - [Narrator] And airlines are constantly recalibrating their programs to keep their economic edge. Whether it's changing the benchmarks for a certain status or increasing the number of miles it costs for a flight, - Airlines have full control over how much a mile is worth and how much to charge for a flight. So, you are kind of at their mercy when you're participating in this program. - [Narrator] Some consumers have called these changes devaluations, drawing scrutiny. In 2024, the Department of Transportation launched a probe into the four largest airline loyalty programs, describing the rewards programs as potentially unfair, deceptive, or anti-competitive. - A lot of people really seem to believe that airlines want to make it as hard as possible to use the miles. And every airline executive ever talked to has said that's not true. - I want people using their miles. I want them to find value when they use their miles. - But I do think there's a frustration when airline miles get devalued. And then suddenly you can't buy the flight that you thought you'd be able to buy because their miles are worthless. - [Narrator] In response, an airline trade group said that loyalty programs are a way for airlines to compete for customers. But the program's impact on consumer decisions may also be dwindling. Research by McKinsey showed that these programs' ability to change flyer's behavior declined between 2017 and 2021, and again between 2021 and 2023. So, in order for loyalty programs to keep their alert, airlines like American are looking to expand the scope of where people can spend and earn miles. - Things that you can buy with cash we want to make available with miles, and we also want to change even the experiences that you can use your miles for. - [Narrator] But like with any change, comes the risk of overcorrection. - We've seen cases at other airlines where they've made big changes and had to sort of walk some of them back, and it's kind of a constant iteration. - [Narrator] Which means that while you might be able to earn miles almost everywhere, how much they can get you is still up for debate. Thanks for watching. If you want to learn more about how loyalty programs have transformed, watch our extended interviews with American Airlines VP of Revenue management and loyalty Scott Chandler and former CEO and chairman Bob Crandall. Click the link here to watch on wsj.com.

- What New Air Force Pilots Go Through In Flight School | Boot Camp | Business Insider: Kind: captions Language: en [Applause] This is drop night. A long anticipated event, where soon to be Air Force Flight School graduates find out what aircraft they will fly. But before they get there, yeah, they have to start here at Laughlin Air Force Base in Del Rio, Texas, just 9 miles north of the US Mexico border, newly commissioned officers begin a year-long journey to earn their wings. The purpose of undergraduate pilot training is to give students experience in an aircraft and in the military aviation world. So, usually they come in with a little bit of flight experience in the civilian world and then we teach them what that's like to do that as a military aviator. The Air Force has grappled with a pilot shortage for the last few years. In response, it's working to streamline training to get new pilots into the cockpit faster without sacrificing standards. After graduating T6s, within a year, they'll be mission ready and could possibly see combat in less than 18 months. Pilots have trained at Laughlin for over 70 years, beginning in World War II when it was a training site for bomber crews. Today, it's home to the 47th Flying Training Wing, and it trains more Air Force pilots than any other base. It's pretty competitive, so it all starts uh either in OTS, ROC, or any other commissioning source that they may come through there. They have to compete to be in the top half of their graduating class. Then there they'll get picked to become a pilot. To be selected, applicants must hold a 4-year college degree, be commissioned as an officer, and pass a series of academic and flight aptitude tests. My degree in college uh was communication studies. I have a hospitality degree from the University of Central Florida. I know it doesn't translate, but during that time, I gathered about 2,000 hours of flight instruction time. I got my private pilots license in high school and then I went to the Air Force Academy and did a couple airmanship things there and then in general been exposed to aviation pretty much my whole life. It's been a lot of years. I'm 27 years old now and I've been told no many times. Uh I've applied several times to get into this program. All students begin training on the T6 Texan 2, a single engine turborop plane designed to teach the basics of military flying. But only top performers are selected to continue training in the T38 Talon, a twin engine supersonic jet that prepares pilots for the fighter jets they may fly in the operational air force. These are all the things that come on when we turn on the avionics master switch that are powered by the generator bus. Before they enter an aircraft cockpit, students spend weeks in the classroom learning its layout, how to read flight instruments, and how weather systems will affect everything they do in the air. So we're going to provide the academic foundation, the theory that we think is essential for all high performance flying that we do here so that when they reach uh the simulator phase of training, they can practice that and then it'll make a lot more sense by the time they get into the aircraft itself. You have to learn about the entire aircraft, how to fly it, where to fly it, and the procedures that follow that. It's a fire hose, so we're getting a lot of information all at once. The pacing is really tough. However, it's manageable and we've been selected for a reason. So, we're here to work hard, study hard, and move on to our bases. So, that's where you're pointed. That's where you're going. Students spend about 100 hours in the academic phase of training before moving to the next phase. logging 50 to 60 hours across three types of simulators to develop skills in cockpit procedures, emergency scenarios, and instrument flying. The unit training device, the UTD, which is a basic cockpit with no visual outside references. It enables the students to practice their cockpit procedures, work on their checklist, and get familiar with the switches and controls. Then they'll upgrade to the instrument flight trainers, which is in a dark container with a simple video screen in front where they can practice developing those instrument skills for landings, for takeoffs, for basic instrument procedures. And then ultimately the the grand finale is the operational flight training which is about 120 degrees of visuals where they can practice ground references, aerobatics, more advanced emergency procedures. Right. But the way you jerk your head right there. Each student is paired with an instructor pilot or IP who evaluates their performance through each phase of training. So, flying the sims, there's certain moments where it it feels really real. Your spatial orientation, how far you're off the ground, emergency procedures. I had that sim last night, and it really picks up tempo, and it's like, oh, wow, this is actually happening. I was here previously as an instructor here in about 2014 to 2018, and it took students about 20 rides to solo. And now utilizing the immersive training devices, they solo in about half the time. So it's really accelerated their development and their growth and ultimately we see a better product from T6's based on that technology. It's a onetoone recreation of what is outside here at Laughlin. So we can practice on the simulators for hours on end, see what our visual references are, and then the first day we step in the plane, we can point out everything that we're familiar with up to that point. While simulators have sped up training, they're just one part of a larger strategy. The Air Force has struggled for the past 8 years to meet its annual goal of producing 1,500 new pilots. There are multiple reasons why. There's always some event that happens, whether it's OBOG's event and the T6. Laughlin had a hail event here several years ago that destroyed basically every T1. They had to be rebuilt. At Laughlin, our IPs are working very hard. You know, typically flying two sorties per day to make sure that we can produce pilots to fill the cockpit that America needs to go defend our national security. One of the most dangerous challenges pilots face is spatial disorientation. A condition where the body's senses can no longer accurately determine the aircraft's position, altitude, or motion. So, if we're flying in formation through the weather, you're really you're just staring at your flight lead's wing. You're not looking at any instruments. They are looking at their instruments, and you're just staring at their plane. And that can become quite disorienting. Tango 62, turn right, heading 120. descend and maintain 6,000 ft. To help them recognize and recover from it, students undergo training in a spatial disorientation simulator. Yeah. So, that was the corololis illusion. And in the aircraft, the coral solution occurs in situations where the aircraft is turning, rolling, or changing pitch. Students also train with the Baron chair, a spinning device that simulates the types of illusions that spatial disorientation can create. It's meant to reinforce an important lesson. Okay. Your senses can lie, but your instruments won't. When you're getting onto it and then you inevitably hit your head, you broke two rules, actually. So, please be careful. We teach them local area survival. And we cover emergency parachute training. Not comfy. Let's help you understand. All right. So, what do we do? need to thumbs downers up higher. There you go. Now pulling pull. But we go through a practical application of what's taught in the class because obviously we're not going to have the opportunity to real world use this until it is a life ordeath situation. And this is a refresher training that you have to do every 36 months to maintain your currency because again it's not things that you're doing every single day. You're going to lose that muscle memory. It's going to feel snug. You should be able to fit your fist between your chest and the chest strap. Squat and pull as evenly as possible. And it should now be easy to send them straight. Before students step into the cockpit, they need to be properly fitted for the gear that will allow them to withstand the intense G forces they will experience in the air. They are going to get custom fit to a helmet, harness, guit. The harness is what is attaching them to their parachute and to their seat kit. Mask is going to provide them aircraft oxygen. The helmet offers basic head protection is also a device we use to mount things like their visor and their mask to their face. Deep breath in. Hold. And exhale. You don't find any air leaking out from up here? You do? Okay. Go ahead and adjust your mask a bit. Every 120 days, we'll check the fit to make sure everything's still fitting well. If they've gained or lost any weight, there's a chance we'll need to tighten or loosen the guit. Laughlin Air Force Base has a fleet of over 160 aircraft, 103 T6s and 59 T38s, valued at more than $800 million, requiring a $1.6 billion maintenance budget. And with three runways, it's one of the Air Force's busiest airfields. They'll be able to text somebody. All right, copy. I'll take care of you. Approximately 450 student pilots are being trained at any given time. And their hands-on training begins in the T6. Man, I love the T6. She's kind of allpurpose, all weather. She can go wherever you want. She's not the fastest or can she hold the most gas, but she goes upside down. She rolls. She pulls. You get all of the training that you would need in the T6 to go off into any other aircraft, whether that be a fighter aircraft, a bomber, a tanker, or a heavy cargo plane. Once you punch that power up, it was definitely surprising. Lots of power, 1100 shaft horsepower compared to the Cessna 172 that you apply at a local flight school. Once in the T6, students begin applying the fundamentals from the classroom and the simulators. This phase of training is broken into three progressive stages. Transition, navigation, and formation. In transition phase, they're looking to pull high G uh high speed maneuvering, other things like that. Uh versus the navigation phase. Uh it's more so slow maneuvering uh methodical process. For the formation phase, again, that high G maneuvering comes into play as well as that slow maneuvering. The reason they call it the transition phase is because finally we're stepping out of these big boxes we call sims. And then once you hop into the jet for the first time, uh for me it's been a six-year goal of mine. So it's a great feeling uh to finally get control of the aircraft, feel the power, and learn how to become a military aviator. Training objectives today will be zero missed ground checklist items/ callouts. Every flight starts with a mission brief. Maintain minus 0 plus 100 ft on the localizer. Students will come to the brief. They've obviously studied before. We'll cover all the basics of the flight. So going through the weather, operational risk management, we'll have a brief overview of the plan uh that we'll run through so that everybody's on the same page and that's standardized between all briefs. I woke up around 4:30 just to start reviewing the weather, start preparing for your flight and just to make sure that you've chair flew the maneuvers. Nothing's going to surprise you and you want to become to the brief as prepared as possible. After the brief, the student and instructor pilots suit up for their flight or sorty and make their way to the step desk. Yep. Briefed and mitigated. Quality. Excellent. Where an operations supervisor will assess their risk level for the flight. We write down everything that might be a risk factor for our flight. So, if we didn't sleep well, if we're not feeling hydrated, if we have personal things going on at home, maybe. And it's just to take a second to look at it, add up the points, be like, "Hey, are we safe to be flying? Does this make sense?" Thank you, man. So, just started flying. We've been in formal for about 4 weeks now, but very first week of flying. Having a blast so far. Our plan was just to go up, do some mow work, basic arobatics, get the blood flowing, get the body pumping and then we're going to head back and recover via localizer. Localizer is an instrument approach you'll do and we typically will practice in VFR which is visual flight rules and IFR which is instrument flight rules. The reason we do that is to practice when the weather is terrible and you can't see the runway. That will give you the lateral course you need in order to get you down to the runway. Once you come back and start debriefing is when you have the time to kind of understand how I could do this better. The debrief is the most critical part to learning. We'll discuss, do we have any safety of flights, right? So, anything where that could have been dangerous, we could have uh gotten in an unsafe situation or maybe something uh not totally legal in the air, right? So, we'll talk about that and then we'll go through like, hey, did we actually meet the objectives that we set today? When you're on the ground talking with your IP, going over what you did and how you can make things better is when you really learn how to become a a great pilot. student pilots that are getting ready to be winged pilots, do not forget how important your manganese brothers and sisters are, cuz without them, you're not doing the mission. So, today was kind of the start of our graduation experience where we get our wings. The tradition was to get someone who gives you the wings that like meant something to you and got you here. And so, today maintenance gave them to us. It's just kind of a symbolic thing to show that they were the ones that really supported us getting the graduation today. Going to Altus Air Force Base in Oklahoma. So, I'll train with the C17 squadron out there. After this, I'm going to Little Rock, Arkansas for 6 months to learn how to fly the C130H. And after that, I'm going to Cheyen, Wyoming to fly for the Wyoming Air National Guard. Students selected for the fighter track, remain at Laughlin for 4 to 6 months. I'm going to go fly the T38, which is the next phase of training here. So, I will stick around Laughlin for a little bit. The students we get for 38s are typically towards the top of their T6 class. So higher performers and T6s and people that want to go fly something fast and pointy. At the end of the day, at the end of the program, their scores goes into what's called a mass and they're ranked number one to the last person and there's only a few slots for both T38s and the major weapon systems and the top students get their top choices. While T38 training follows the same structure as T6, the pace is faster, the demands are greater, and the margin for error is smaller. T38, you really got to be on top of your game. There's a lot of things that you have to be constantly thinking about and making decisions on. It's like, are you on air speed, altitude, and you're doing like maneuvering out in the MOA or anything? It flies very different than a T6, so they need to learn how to land it, how to take off, how to do all the ground operations, and even get the jet started to begin with. Uh, so we'll start with those things. We'll start just flying out to some airspace, practicing loops or simple arrow, and just practicing landing, practicing landing in different configurations. And then we're going right into formation phase, which I think is really just the crux of the entire program. There are so many great benefits to formation flying. It simulates the stress and the fear of a deployed location, right? We don't have bombs coming at us, but we have to in some way uh instill the pressure from the outside world. And I think formation is a great way to start. So the 38 uh in formation itself is a little bit different than the T6. The T6 you got about 10 to 20 foot spacing between the aircraft due to the wing length. The 38 you got about 3 ft. So you're a lot closer, you're a lot faster, and there's a lot more going on that you have to pay attention to. No matter what aircraft they go on, they're going to fly some type of formation. Whether that be two C130s following two minutes behind each other or if you have a boom sticking out the back of your tanker with a fighter aircraft rejoining to refuel. [Music] But not everyone gets the assignment they want. Some graduates will be selected to become first assignment instructor pilots or FAPES. They'll spend four years training students to fly the T6 or the T38. So today is our graduation ceremony for our pie training class. It's the final step where we get our wings, we get to pin them on, and we officially become pilots in the Air Force. How it feels for me is uh kind of surreal. Um my father was a pilot in the uh US Air Force. My grandfather worked for the Air National Guard and then my uncle was uh an officer in the Air Force. And having that uh history of uh you know my family members being in the military, it just kind of reinstates that uh that drive that I had uh when I first set out to join the Air Force and when I first set out to actually push forward to be a pilot. It finally feels so good to be at this moment. We put in a lot of hard work over these last six, seven months, and to finally have it come to an end is bittersweet, but it's pretty fun. These newly pinned pilots will head to their next base to begin aircraft specific training. Some will fly tankers, others fighters, bombers, or cargo planes, but they'll all leave Laughlin with the same title, United States Air Force Pilot.

  13. @youssifganni: I don’t know why this makes me happy for them new pilots, but pains me. It has always been a dream to have such a beautiful system in Canada, but this is far from being the case. Because for US and especially Canadian (my case) citizens, it is never that simple to become an airline pilot. First of all, all Canadian pilots deal with garbage weather including snow. But also, 80-95% of us would never even come close of touching the controls of an Airbus even with 1500 hours on single engines. After acquiring 750-1000 hours minimum on Cessnas/Pipers as flight instructors (2-3 years), we then apply for Northern Air Taxi companies where we start flying multi piston aircrafts, if lucky, turbo props. Within 1-3 years of flying with instruments and a minimum of 500 hours PIC (pilot in command) time, we can then consider applying for regional airlines. Not even mainlines (international). From which we would spend another 2-4 years. Then we could apply for mainline in which we will take a big pay cut. So technically this is a process that takes between 6-8 years. Some will do pay to fly which can cost an upwards of 100k USD abroad. But not all have that luxury. Hence why we have so much shortage of pilots here in Canada and in the US. And why we are missing experienced ones. I graduated in 2019 with a college diploma and ATPL frozen, not only that, trained on a level 3 ascent simulator on a 737, 2 years experience northern flying on turboprops, yet, no company will bat an eye at my CV despite having way more experience than those entry level pilots.
