- What It Takes to Become an Air Force Test Pilot: Kind: captions Language: en - [ATC] Affirm, flyby tower is ready. - [EVIL] Cobra 17, downwind, flyby 450. - [Automated Voice] Altitude, pull up, pull up. (jet aircraft roaring) - [Sam] That's our "Top Gun" moment for sure right there. (dramatic music) - It's an experience definitely that breaks you down, but you all get built back up together and it's a beautiful thing to watch. - We're taking experienced aviators and engineers and teaching them how to fly anything. (airplanes whirring) (helicopter whirring) - Whether we put it on a spacecraft or an aircraft is kind of immaterial, the science behind it is the same. (dramatic synth music) (airplane whooshing) - I'm here in the Mojave Desert at Edwards Air Force Base to find out what it takes to become one of the world's most skilled pilots. This is where aviation history was rewritten, where the sound barrier was first shattered, where the space shuttle first flew and where the limits of what's possible continue to be pushed to new heights. I'll be bringing you along to see how test pilots are trained, what makes this school so prestigious, and at the end of it all, I'll be gearing up for a backseat ride in an F-16 Viper to experience real test pilot maneuvers. So strap in and get ready, because this is an inside look at the Air Force's elite Test Pilot School. (jet aircraft whooshing) What it takes to get into the test pilot school, the bar is very, very high. - The hardest part about test pilot school is doing all the things at once. - We are getting through a two-year master's and a one-year flying program in 12 months. - They are, without question, the best of the best. (aircraft canopy whirring) In the military, we're constantly in this cat and mouse game with potential adversaries. We're always pushing the boundary of technology. How can we make sure that they're effective, that they're safe, they're trustworthy, that they're ethical and also capable. The test pilot school teaches people how to test new things so that the war fighter doesn't have to. When they climb in the aircraft, they know everything from the life support system to the weapons. The aircraft itself will work. (inspiring music) - This is the place, no kidding, where we're all here to make sure that the only one surprised when it's time to fight is the adversary. - We're not teaching people how to fly for the first time. We're taking experienced - What a view. When you walk through the halls of Air Force Test Pilot School, it really does feel like you're walking in the footsteps of aviation legends. Names like Chuck Yeager, who became the first pilot to break the sound barrier in level flight. Neil Armstrong, the first man on the moon, flew over 200 aircraft during his time here at Edwards, including the hypersonic rocket-powered X-15 that still to this day is one of my favorites. (intense music) Between 1962 and 1975, the test pilot school expanded its role to include astronaut training for its test pilots. - [Astronaut] Certainly has to be the most (speaks faintly). - 37 test pilot school graduates were selected from the different services for the US space program, and 26 earned their astronaut wings flying for programs like the X-15, Gemini, Apollo and Space Shuttle. And speaking of the Space Shuttle, Edwards was the perfect place to conduct those initial flights. (gentle piano music) Rogers Dry Lake bed, which is less than a mile from where we are right now at the heart of Edwards Air Force Base, features the world's longest runway, which comes in at a staggering 12,000 meters or roughly 40,000 feet. And actually, the very first space shuttle mission STS-1 landed back here at Edwards Air Force Base on runway 17 after completing 54 orbits around Earth. (bright piano music) - So this is a Commandant row. It shows the pictures of all the commandants here at the test pilot school, and you're looking at, you know, a wall of luminaries. The people who stand out the most here are Chuck Yeager. So back when he was Colonel Chuck Yeager, he did so much to advance the space education here at the school, ultimately flying the NF-104, as you might have seen in the movie, "The Right Stuff." And another obviously well-known luminary and leader in the space and aviation community is Buzz Aldrin. So you can see here, he was a commandant at the school after he got back from the moon landing. And again, just to be in the presence and the company of these men and women who have led the school is really an honor. (trainees chattering) - [Pilot] We'll just do that. - So each year we have hundreds of applicants from across all the major weapons systems in the Air Force who apply. And of those, we ultimately end up picking about 24. And they're split between two different classes. One class that'll start every January and another class that'll start every July. The class here lasts 12 months, and we can break that into three main phases. The first is the performance phase. During that phase, the students are learning fundamentals of tests in addition to the most basic kind of testing, which is how fast, how far, how high, how many Gs can this aircraft perform or pull. The second phase is focused on the flying qualities and handling qualities of an aircraft. We are teaching our students how to look at an aircraft to size it up and understand how it's going to fly in terms of its qualities. The last phase that we focus on is the mission systems. So all the elements of the aircraft that actually allow to perform its function, whether it's the radar, the electronic warfare suite, weapons delivery, communications and navigation and so on. The hands-on, the academics, the real-world test project. The hardest part about test pilot school - My background is in aerospace engineering and so I was not shocked about the academic rigor. I was shocked about how compressed this timeline is. We are getting through a two-year master's and a one year flying Days usually start around 5:00 AM and go well beyond the 12-hour mark. It's an experience definitely - And I think it's the sum total of managing all of that and knowing what to prioritize at this moment. That really constitutes the major challenge of the school. Individual students will get to fly something about 20 to 25 different aircraft. - Aircraft that are being tested right now that we probably won't hear about for years, and if you can imagine are pretty crazy. - That includes everything from fighter aircraft like the F-16 to prop aircraft, the C-12, the Osprey, the R-44 and the Black Hawk, T-38, P-51 Mustang to the B-25. - And it's really to beat into us that we need to be comfortable in uncomfortable situations. - [ATC] Level 20 inches, break the nose up here, perfect. - So if we are the first to fly in a novel weapon system that we can do so safely, securely and effectively every time. (intense dramatic music) - What's so fascinating about test pilots is that they're behind the controls of some of the latest and greatest technology in military aviation. But there is one aircraft here that has been making headlines worldwide, an experimental platform designed to test artificial intelligence in dog fighting. It's a project that's been pretty secretive around here but one that we were given special permission to show you. So get ready for the X-62 Vista. - So Sam, what your viewers are looking at here in the background is the X-62 Vista. This is an incredible, one of a kind, autonomy fighter testbed. It began life as an F-16, specifically for testing thrust vectoring on the F-22, and actually did the initial test that ultimately got to almost 180 degrees of alpha, which is an incredible accomplishment. We ultimately upgraded it to install AI computers so that we could determine how effective AI can be in combat. - And I know everyone's wondering, but can you answer who won that dog fight? - Well, I can't tell you whether or not it won in the air. What I can say is that it was effective enough that we're continuing to do research and advance new lines of research for combat autonomy. - So I see the big dorsal fin pretty much running the entire length of the fuselage. Is that the only structural modification on Vista? - So Vista actually began life as the NF-16 and it actually came off the line with several modifications. All the computers that run the autonomy live in that dorsal fin. And in addition, as you can see, the paint scheme is a little bit different than a usual F-16. - Yeah. - One of the things that stands out is the orange here, which is an homage to test. So early testers would wear orange flight suits in order to be, you know, safely recovered if they had to eject out of aircraft. And really since that time, we've used orange as the test color. You can see it at TPS on our patches. - So how are the AI models actually trained? Like, how does this aircraft learn to dog fight autonomously? - There are these models that engineers and scientists, part of DARPA and other research labs, who put together and they've created an environment where you can simulate dog fighting one-v-one, two-v-two, many-v-many, and you can run it much, much faster than real-time. And those models are not perfect. There are things that they're not gonna capture about winds, about altitude, about the aircraft itself. - So as the expert, do you see a world in the near future where AI replaces pilots? Autonomy takes over, what's your take? - Well, the purpose of this test is really to identify how useful combat AI can be. We know from our day-to-day life, right, that there are areas where AI is going to perform better than humans and other areas where it's not gonna perform nearly as well. We're gonna keep pushing the technology to find out just how far we can take it. My suspicion is that the real value will demonstrate itself when humans and AI are both doing the things that they're best at in combination with each other. - Now, even though this is test pilot school, that doesn't mean everything is about flying. It's about pushing the limits of aviation and expanding into other domains as well. Here, students aren't just pilots. They're engineers, they're analysts, they're innovators. And part of the curriculum includes taking lessons in space where students get to fly actual satellites. And all of that happens right here at the space operations control room. - It's important for us to understand how our satellites operate, if we're gonna be able to defend them against adversary threats. To get after that multi-domain testing, we wanna have the space folks working with the flight folks the same way that the satellites are supporting the airplanes in a war fight. So Sam, we've got a unique opportunity for you here today to operate a satellite that is actually on orbit. It's the XVI satellite which is about twice the size of this satellite. So just think this and then attach, put some solar array sticking out of it and a two-meter antenna sticking out. You're gonna be changing the orientation of that satellite. - Okay. - You're just slewing it. - And slewing means going from what, one position to another. - To another. - Okay, now honestly, I was pretty surprised to hear that the Space Force is a part of test pilot school, but when you think about it, it makes perfect sense. Space is a war fighting domain, just like the air. That's why the yearlong space test course blends fundamentals like leadership and problem-solving with specialized training in orbital mechanics, astrodynamics and satellite systems. And just like the pilots in the sky, guardians are learning how to test, innovate and push the boundaries of what's possible. - Future conflicts aren't going to be in just one domain, right? We're gonna have troops fighting on the ground, we're gonna have planes in the air, we're gonna have ships at sea and we're gonna have satellites and orbits supporting all of them. Whether we put it on a spacecraft So we teach the space and the flight students that stuff together. All right, and real-time commands went up. We're now at 182, and we can see that green line jumped again and that will continue ticking on. So you've basically told the satellite to do a pirouette now. - Now the biggest takeaway for me is that you can be a part of something as legendary as test pilot school while serving in the Space Force. As a guardian, your mission is to secure our nation's interests in, from and to space. And if that sounds like something you might be interested in, you can head to spaceforce.com to start your journey today. - My absolute favorite part of this job is working with the instructors at the test pilot school who are, without question, They are, without a doubt, extraordinary. So a big part of of being a test pilot, especially for fighter type aircraft, is understanding how to test an airplane, what we call departure characteristics. Now a good way to think of this is imagine you're driving a car and you drive on an IC patch and all of a sudden the the backend swings out and now you're doing 360 down the road. That's a departure from controlled flight in a car. Now in an airplane when you depart from controlled flight, you do in three dimensions and you typically do at much higher speed. You're higher but you need a lot more altitude to recover as well. So we have to teach them not only how to fly and how to determine how an aircraft handles near those limits, but once they go beyond the limits to determine whether or not they can recover. And that's a really unusual way to depart, because you run out of air speed, and when you're out of air speed, you're floating in the cockpit. It's like you're on zero G. And right about here, we're starting to fall backwards, and as we gain speed falling backwards, we'll just start to flop forward a little bit and there she goes. And we'll just kind of let her settle out. She'll probably self recover, and there's the recovery. So the entire airplane, if it's rolling really rapidly, if we push on it with a tail, so let's say we're in a really rapid roll, we push on it with a tail, all those gyroscopic forces will cause it to yaw out. We're gonna start by getting slow but not too slow, because we need to have enough air speed to really make the airplane roll fast. So we're gonna be rolling over and over and over again. I just gotta give me a little straight low for a bit and then we're good. - [Bill] I've had more people, you know, get sick to stuff like this, but nobody ever wished they hadn't done it. - [Sam] Yeah, that's very true. - [Bill] Flyby tower, Cobra 17. - Affirm, flyby tower is ready. - So the tower flyby is an interesting maneuver, because you end up flying very low and very fast. But why would we fly 100 feet above the ground and up to almost supersonic speeds? Well, as it turns out, as you go faster and slower in an airplane, the instruments that show your altitude, the altimeter, they change. Well, that needs to be corrected. So we need to learn what those errors are. So the best way to do it is we just put some engineers in a tower. They've got a device that exactly measures our height above the ground and we go flying a given height, about 100-feet over the ground and a range of air speeds. They measure a height above the ground and then we calculate the difference between that measured height and then the height by instruments show. And we use that to create corrections for the airplane. Cobra 17, downwind, flyby, 450 - [ATC] Cobra 17 flight, report base tower flyby. 718, wind 200, report base flyby. - [Bill] More Gs to line up on this. Flight 17, flyby, 450. We're gonna lower here. It is 450, we've got 150 feet. The beautiful thing about flight tests for me is first of all, every day is a little bit different, because we're always testing new things, trying new things. It's kind of amazing. And ready, ready. Pedal power. - That's our "Top Gun" moment for sure right there. Let's go! - [Bill] Escort Cobra 17, we're completing the up in the area array to RTB for a high key SFO. - [ATC] Cobra 17, roger on request. - What it takes to get into the test pilot school? The the bar is very, very high. All of our students are very intelligent. In fact, it's difficult for them, because they're all used to being very high in their classes all through their lives. They show up at test pilot school and they discover pretty quickly that half of 'em are below average. But they're all very intelligent. They work very hard, they all really want to do the job. It's like the perfect mix of teaching a subject I love to people that would love to learn it. We're doing it in airplanes. I mean, it's perfect for me. - Well, that's a wrap here at test pilot school. And what a genuinely incredible experience. This isn't just about flying jets, it's about pushing the limits of what's possible, testing new technology and shaping the future of aviation. After that flight, I can confidently say that being a test pilot isn't for everyone. It takes a tremendous amount of skill and a mindset built for constant challenges. But for those that make it through, they've certainly earned their place in history as a true aviation pioneer. Thanks so much for coming along for this ride. And I'll catch you next time.

Estamos monitoreando las comunicaciones de control de tráfico aéreo Monitorearemos eso Miraremos por la ventana para asegurarnos de que no estemos volando en tormentas o ese tipo de cosas Buscamos atajos estables si no están ahí Una vez por hora, revisaremos todas las partes del sistema para asegurarnos de que todo esté funcionando correctamente Después de despegar con buen clima pudimos experimentar cómo los pilotos vuelan a través de una tormenta de truenos Así es como se ve en el avión No volaríamos por la parte roja Ni siquiera nos gusta volar por la parte verde Así que rotaríamos. Le pediríamos al control de tráfico aéreo un giro a 150 Podemos hacer eso a bordo Ahora vamos a empezar a girar a la izquierda Y aquí viene la turbulencia Sigue girando Por eso es que a veces el piloto automático hace un trabajo mejor Es un poco más estable aunque no es tan rápido El simulador pone a los pilotos a prueba en casos de fallas en el motor hasta tormentas severas o choques con aves United no nos mostró cómo se ven los choques en el simulador Pero los pilotos practican éstas situaciones de emergencia cada nueve meses Lo bueno es que no estamos haciendo ese tipo de cosas en el mundo real muy seguido pero queremos asegurarnos de que nuestra tripulación esté lista para lidiar con cualquier cosa Cuando yo intenté aterrizar, fue obvio que no soy piloto Contenlo un poco Eso fue mi culpa Manejar un simulador fue mucho más difícil de lo que pensé y mucho mas realista Pero el centro no es solo para pilotos 1,500 de los 24,000 asistentes de vuelo de la aerolínea también acuden al centro una vez al año para aprender y mantenerse al día sobre los procedimientos de emergencia Esta es nuestra balsa de deslizamiento aquí en United Airlines y aquí es donde cada nuevo piloto tiene que deslizarse al menos una vez en su carrera al igual que las azafatas, quiénes lo deben hacer una vez como mínimo Y esperamos que esta sea la única vez que tengan que hacer algo como esto ¿Estás lista? Da un paso adelante y para abajo vas Siéntate, siéntate Buen trabajo Los asistentes de vuelo de United también reciben entrenamientos anuales para aprender a lidiar con situaciones médicas y evacuar aviones Quitate el cinturón de seguridad, sal, deja todo El objetivo que tenemos en United es que hay un marco de tiempo de 90 segundos para evacuar a todos los pasajeros del avión de manera segura para sobrevivir Te voy a guiar a través de cada paso Haz eso hacia arriba Ahora vas a mirar afuera ¿Ves fuego, humo, escombros u obstrucciones? No Entonces, adelantes. Esa palanca grande hace cosas grandes Gira la palanca, suéltala y agárrate como lo hiciste ¡Vengan por aquí! En tus propias palabras eso funcionaría porque no estás capacitada como asistente de vuelo Con todos estos recursos y réplicas para pilotos, auxiliares de vuelo y técnicos United ha invertido una suma considerable en su entrenamiento El centro cuenta con más de mil millones y medio de dólares en bienes en la propiedad United dice que estos protocolos de entrenamiento son estándar en la industria Si todos volamos al nivel más seguro, nos beneficia a todos Competiremos vigorosamente en producto y servicio pero no competiremos en seguridad Después de ver las instalaciones y qué tan riguroso es el entrenamiento Me siento segura de que los pilotos están tan bien entrenados, que están listos para cualquier cosa sin importar lo que pase

- How To Become a Pilot (Step-By-Step Guide): Kind: captions Language: en career if you want to get paid to fly you will need at least a second class medical certificate and if you plan to become an airline pilot you will need a first class medical I recommend that you get one of those prior to starting Flight Training to prevent any surprises down the line most people pass the medical exam without an issue but some medical conditions can prevent you from becoming a pilot one of the most common concern is color vision deficiency now Color Blind Pilots can still fly but they're usually restricted to Flying recreationally and only during the day so if you want to make a career out of flying you will need to pass a color vision test if you're concerned about color blindness or other health conditions we've linked additional information in the description below your Amame also can address some of the concerns you may have we've also added a link down here to help you find an am near you okay so you meet the medical requirements what's next well you need to choose a flight school this is one of the most important decision you will make in your flying career if you make the wrong choice it could end your dream entirely here's what you need to know about selecting a flight school the first thing to consider is whether to go for a part 61 or part 141 flight school basically there are two types of flight schools and different rules apply to them in general part 61 schools offer more flexible training programs on the other hand part 141 schools are more rigid and structured you can get your license faster at a part 141 flight school but they are more expensive to learn more check out this video right here where I explain the difference in detail another consideration is the distance between your home and the flight school now keep in mind that your commute could add a significant amount of time to your training so plan accordingly next consider the type of airplane that the flight school operates the most important thing make sure that the airplanes are in good condition and wellmaintained newer aircraft are an advantage especially if they have glass cockpits but you don't need to fly the latest and greatest to get your license there's definitely a premium to newer aircraft step three after you choose a flight school you'll need to choose an instructor a lot of students don't even think about choosing a fight instructor this is a big mistake the wrong instructor can make or break your flight training experience here's how to make the right choice first make a list of the flight schools near you visit a few of them and then speak with the instructors are they professional enthusiastic welcoming that's a great sign a happy instructor is a good instructor or do the instructors look like they haven't slept in day and maybe wear a permanent frown well you might want to look elsewhere in this case now consider doing an intro flight at several flight schools this is the first flight you will have before officially beginning your training it usually consists of a scening route with an instructor and it's a great way to get a good sense of how flight school operates it's also a good idea to take multiple intro flights with several instructors at different flight schools before you make a choice also don't hesitate to request different instructors early on if you feel like they're not a good fit once you've chosen your flight school and instructor it's time to start Flight Training now this can be a little bit scary especially if you don't know what you expect now let's talk about the structure of a typical flight lesson first your instructor will give you an overview of the lesson it's very important that you prepare four lessons ahead of time then you'll both head out to the airplane what you do during the flight depends on the lesson but there is a general structure your instructor will typically demonstrate a procedure or maneuver then you will try to do it on your own now you'll continue doing that until they are happy with your performance now I expect a postflight briefing at the end of the lesson you're a new instructor will discuss your performance what you did well and also what needs Improvement this is also a good time to learn about your next flight now with each flight and ground lesson you get closer to earning your private pirate certificate the one that will allow you to fly with passengers without getting paid when your instructor is confident in your abilities they'll endorse you for for your first solo which is exactly what it sounds like you will be flying the airplane on your own for the first time very exciting after you start flying solo it's time to think about taking your knowledge test now this is the first official test that you will need to take so what does the test consist of well the Private Pilot Knowledge Test has 60 multiple choice questions you will have 2 hours to complete it and you need to score 70% or higher so here's what you should do to pass with flying colors the key is prepar ation don't expect to read just a few books and then go pass the test now luckily that's where we can help our Private Pilot Made Easy online Ground School is designed to help you pass the exam we're so confident that we'll even send you $175 and a full refund if you don't pass on the first try now click on the link in the description to learn more you're almost there after you've passed your Knowledge Test the only thing left is an in-person test with a designated pilot examiner or DPE DPE is an exper examiner approved by the FAA their job is to make sure that you are qualified proficient and a safe pilot this in-person test is also known as a practical test or more commonly a check ride the check ride has an oral and a flight portion the oral exam happens on the ground and consists of a conversation between you and the DPE they're going to evaluate your Aviation knowledge your flight planning and the decision- making skills it can range anywhere between an hour and a half to 3 hours now when your DPE is happy with your knowledge it's time to hop in the aircraft during the flight portion you will demonstrate various skills Maneuvers and that usually takes about an hour the DP wants to see you operate the aircraft safely and with Precision it will also test your decision-making abilities in the aircraft if the DP is happy with your skills you will become the country's newest pilot congratulations this is the first step on the way to becoming an airline pilot so how long does it take to get your private pirate certificate well if you're training full-time can be around 3 to 6 months you'll need a minimum of 40 hours of flight time but the average person takes about 70 to 80 hours it really depends on the weather the type of airspace that you learn in how consistently you fly and in many other factors okay so you're pilot but you can't get paid to fly just yet before you get your commercial pilot certificate you need to obtain flying experience and an instrument rating here's how an instrument rating allows you to fly an aircraft in limited visibility using only the instruments in the cockpit similar to the private pattern process you'll need to pass a written and a practical test good news if your current fly instructor can provide instrument training you can continue to fly with them the experience is pretty much the same as your Private Pilot training just more complex Concepts after you get your instrument rating you're almost there the bad news well now you need to get a commercial pirot certificate the good news the process is very similar to your Private Pilot certificate there's a knowledge test and a check ride that follows the same format we just discussed the biggest difference is the level of difficulty the commercial Knowledge Test covers much more than the private pirate test and the check ride is way more strict but what makes the commercial certificate especially difficult is how much experience you need for starters you'll need at least 250 hours of flight time now at a part 141 flight school you only need 190 hours but it still takes a significant amount of time and money to achieve once you pass the commercial check ride you can finally get paid to fly if you train full-time you can expect to obtain your commercial certificate in about 6 to 18 months keep in mind that this can vary depending on the weather and the flight scool now it's time to go straight to the airlines right well not exactly before you fly the big jets you need more hours under your belt that's why many commercial pilots become flying structures it's a fantastic way to quickly gain experience at this point you should also get your multi-engine tring this allows you to fly aircraft with more than one engine But ultimately you need an airline transport pilot certificate in order to fly for an airline and that requires 1,500 hours of flight time now many inspiring airline pilots gain those hours by instructing some Pilots also find other commercial flying jobs that don't require an ATP many do a bit of both once you've earned that ATP certificate your dream is finally Within Reach and now you can start to apply for Airline jobs it can take anywhere from 2 to 5 years in order to obtain that ATP certificate depending on the type of flying that you do now you may say what is this going to cost Well Medical certificate can cost anywhere between $75 to $200 depending on the type of certificate and also the doctor your Private Pilot certificate will average $ 15 to $20,000 this also varies significantly depending on the flight school type of aircraft the instructor or even the location the commercial pilot certificate is the most expensive expect around $55,000 this can even go into six figures depending on the flight school you should also consider the cost of obtaining your flight instructure certificate now this is not a requirement but it's one of the best way to gain experience before you go to the airlines a fly instructor rating will cost around $5,000 the good news is that once you are employed as a fly instructor or you can start to get paid to fly you flight school might even pay for additional upgrades to your instructor rating and finally your ATP certificate some Airlines might pay for your ATP but if you have to pay for it yourself it will cost around $5,000 now I know you're probably a little - Wish I knew this before starting flight training #2025: Kind: captions Language: en becoming a pilot is one of the most rewarding things you will ever do and it will open up a new world of wonderful experiences however everything wor doing has some challenges and I've heard from a few of my local students and from several of you through email that you might be struggling while there are no silver bullets here are six things I've learned that I believe would have made my journey less stressful hopefully they'll be helpful to you let me start with a question if you ask someone that knows you really well would they say you're good at planning there's a fair amount of research that shows that most people are not as good as they believe while occasionally things go as planned the majority of the time they don't this is why my wife rightfully teases me about always being home late from a lesson or a trip this happens because we don't know and we can't control all of the variables things like weather maintenance issues needing to work around other people's schedules they can all get in the way of completing things as planned because of that I've learned it is better to try and plan pessimistically and be pleasantly surprised can plan optimistically and be unpleasantly surprised here's a related question how long do you think it will take to earn your Private Pilot certificate the FAA requires 40 hours of preparation and many pilot candidates measure themselves against that 40-hour metric but 40 hours is the bare minimum in the USA the average time it takes for someone to complete their training is between 65 and 70 hours almost 2 times the FAA minimums the actual amount of time it will take will depend on a lot of factors including your ability to absorb information your ability to accept and act on feedback how frequently you can train the complexity of the airspace you're training in the complexity of your aircraft the experience of your instructor and much much more it took me 70 hours to prepare for my Private Pilot check ride and to be honest there were several times during that final 30 hours when I was unfairly questioning my abilities why was it taking me so long and you know what it wasn't helpful in fact I now know that worrying actually increased the time that it should have taken so the second thing I wish I knew is to spend less time worrying about how long training is taking and more time focusing on the journey Third you may have heard that Aviation cliche that it only takes two things to fly air speed and money well well it's meant as a joke but there's a heavy dose of reality behind it in fact Financial constraints are one of the most frequently mentioned reasons for not completing flight training but there are things that you can do about it first you realistic about how much it's going to cost the costs of Flight Training can vary significantly across the country so it might be hard to come up with an estimate most flight schools offer some kind of cost estimate and some are really good but others tend to lean towards the optimistic side if you're interested I've put a link in the comment section to a spreadsheet that can help you create a more realistic estimate second put together a plan about how you're going to come up with the money ask yourself the following questions and be honest with the answer answers first do you really have the money in the bank if not how much more do you need second are you eligible for Grants and scholarships can you work full or part-time while training are there other expenses that you're willing and able to drop in order to fly and finally do you have family willing to help third have a significant amount of the estimated money available before you begin training the amount you should have on hand depends on your current situation the slower you're able to accumulate money the more you should put away before starting why well because running out of money sucks it never happens at a good time it takes your focus off the training materials and it often stops your training cold and gaps in your training are not only frustrating they make your training take longer and therefore make it more expensive a couple of quick notes first there's a a lot of discussion on the web about the dangers or the benefits of taking out loans for Flight Training if you're considering this I would urge caution I've seen many instances where the loans worked well for candidates training to change careers in these cases the cost of the loan was an investment against future earning potential they carefully weighed the pros and cons and for them it made sense I've also seen individuals get themselves into trouble by borrowing money for recreational training or because they didn't carefully read or consider the terms therefore my recommendation would be to consider loans as a last option and suggest that you do your homework very carefully before finalizing any agreements to wrap up this item I want to point out one more thing and that is that having a plan doesn't guarantee you won't run out of money but it definitely lessens the likelihood fourth for me was the realization that I was really going to have to work hard for my certificates when I started out I had no idea how much I'd need to study and practice to become a pilot it was daunting to make things worse I realized that studying and practice wouldn't stop once I became a pilot regul ation change equipment changes and you don't always use all the skills regularly so to remain competent and safe you essentially need to rearn your certificate every 24 months because of this you need to get comfortable studying really dig in when there's something you don't understand consider it a puzzle and reward yourself when you figure it out you know once you get into the habit of studying it becomes a lot easier and at that point it even becomes enjoyable along the same lines was the realization that flight training is a bit art and a bit science and a bit Athletics you need to learn math physics Aviation law regulations geography meteorology and and risk management and then you need to blend them together to safely and professionally pilot an aircraft the variety of knowledge risk and skill elements required make Flight Training very challenging there are things you're going to pick up easily but there are other things that will be really hard and the reality is that there will be at least one time when you've had it and you're ready to quit I call this the pit of despair and if it happens to you remember two things first you're not alone it happens to a lot of pilot candidates it's happened to me more than once and chances are it's even happened to your instructor so don't be afraid to talk with others about it secondly you can get through it it may take additional work and maybe some extra help but if you persevere you will get through it and you will be very proud of yourself because you did finally taking a journey alone is usually not as fun as traveling with other others Flight Training is exactly the same surrounding yourself with like-minded individuals will not only make things easier but more enjoyable too joining an online group is a good start but there's nothing quite like a local in-person study group these folks can make studying easier and more fun give you a built-in group to bounce questions off of hold you accountable and challenge you to try harder and be better help you through the tough times like the pit of despair and last but not least they can and will celebrate with you during each Victory along the way so there you have it six things I wish I knew before I started Flight Training hopefully knowing them will help make your journey just a little bit more predictable and a little less stressful do you have others you'd like to add to the list if so please put them in the comments   13. @DeereX748 [1 likes]: The cost of learning to fly is small when you consider the cost of flying enough once you have your license to be competent and stay that way. Flying is a perishable skill, more than just continued studying and learning new regulations. There is plane rental and fuel cost and insurance, which you have to have money for every time you leave the ground. If all you can afford is once around the pattern every few months, quit while you're ahead because you'll never have the muscle memory or reflex knowledge to be competent and safe. You have to weigh those costs against what you have to have to live, and if you have a family, what you have to have to furnish them with their necessities and still have enough to fly. If you can figure out a way to make money while you fly, then do it. Go ahead and commit the training money to not only get your private license, but get the additional requirements to become an instructor. You build time and skill of your own experience and ability while you get paid to do it while teaching others.
