- Single Pilot Operations: The Risks and Challenges: Kind: captions Language: en You may remember this - or it was well before&nbsp; you were born… but in the early days of aviation,&nbsp;&nbsp; there were five flight crew members in the&nbsp; cockpit assisting in flying the aircraft.&nbsp;&nbsp; These crew members consisted of the captain,&nbsp; the first officer, the flight engineer,&nbsp;&nbsp; the navigator, and a radio operator. With&nbsp; advancements in technology, the crew complement&nbsp;&nbsp; has been now reduced to just two on-duty&nbsp; personnel - the captain and the first officer. Near the end of 2022, we heard the news of&nbsp; some regulators coming together to discuss&nbsp;&nbsp; the possibility of removing one of the pilots&nbsp; in the cockpit and heading toward what is called&nbsp;&nbsp; Single Pilot Operations (SPO). This news&nbsp; was not well taken by most in the industry. So, is SPO a possibility? In this video,&nbsp;&nbsp; we will discuss the main concerns and challenges&nbsp; in implementing SPO in commercial air transport. If single pilot operations were&nbsp; to become a reality - it would&nbsp;&nbsp; be huge for the airlines. Their flight crew&nbsp; requirements would be significantly reduced,&nbsp;&nbsp; and any pilot shortage issues could&nbsp; be solved - or at least postponed. Of course, airlines will always prioritize safety&nbsp; over profits - even if it sometimes doesn’t feel&nbsp;&nbsp; that way from a passenger perspective. Having two&nbsp; pilots instead of one would indeed be an issue of&nbsp;&nbsp; safety. So what are the main concerns associated&nbsp; with having just one pilot flying the aircraft? Well, on the surface, the concerns are&nbsp; obvious. Two pilots in the cockpit have&nbsp;&nbsp; been the norm since the beginning of passenger air&nbsp; transport, and there are many reasons behind it. The first obvious reason for why there&nbsp; should be two pilots in the cockpit is&nbsp;&nbsp; Redundancy - having a back-up pilot&nbsp; who is able to fly the aircraft. If one of the pilots were to become&nbsp; incapacitated, the other pilot could&nbsp;&nbsp; take over the full operation of the aircraft&nbsp; and make a landing. Every so often, we hear&nbsp;&nbsp; reports of pilots becoming incapacitated&nbsp; during a flight. In these situations,&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s more important than ever to have another&nbsp; experienced crew member who is able to cover. If you know anything about&nbsp; aircraft design and operations,&nbsp;&nbsp; one of the pillars of aviation is redundancy&nbsp; - and Taking one pilot away is a big risk. Secondly, a reduction in overall workload for&nbsp; each crew member is the other factor to consider. A pilot’s job is not only to fly the&nbsp; aircraft. It also involves proper&nbsp;&nbsp; systems management and communication&nbsp; to ensure the safety of the operation.&nbsp;&nbsp; In today’s airliner cockpit, there&nbsp; are two pilots with assigned roles.&nbsp;&nbsp; One is the Pilot Flying (PF), and the&nbsp; other is the Pilot Monitoring (PM).&nbsp;&nbsp; The PF’s job is to fly and navigate the aircraft,&nbsp; while the job of the PM is everything else.&nbsp;&nbsp; This includes communication with the ATC, systems&nbsp; monitoring and management, and, most importantly,&nbsp;&nbsp; monitoring the actions of the PF. This way,&nbsp; the most critical part of the flight - the&nbsp;&nbsp; act of flying itself - is centered around one&nbsp; single pilot, which reduces the risk of errors. The roles of the PF and PM intensify in&nbsp; failure conditions, where the PM still&nbsp;&nbsp; manages the non-flying abnormal procedures that&nbsp; are required to counter the failure condition.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is in abnormal conditions that the&nbsp; importance of two pilots is fully realized.&nbsp;&nbsp; Complex failures can be difficult to comprehend,&nbsp; and this requires active input from both pilots&nbsp;&nbsp; to dive deep into the problem and solve&nbsp; it. This sometimes may include shifting&nbsp;&nbsp; of the roles between the pilots (i.e.,&nbsp; the PF momentarily becoming the PM). While much of the movie Sully is a dramatized&nbsp; Hollywood version of the 2009 event known as&nbsp;&nbsp; the “Miracle on the Hudson,” the cockpit&nbsp; scene demonstrates how important a second&nbsp;&nbsp; crew member can be during a crisis, as the&nbsp; captain is able to focus on flying the plane&nbsp;&nbsp; while the first officer carefully&nbsp; goes over the appropriate checklist. The other benefit of two pilot operations&nbsp; is that both pilots can check each other’s&nbsp;&nbsp; work to ensure that something is done&nbsp; properly. As humans do make mistakes,&nbsp;&nbsp; one more set of eyes watching helps to minimize&nbsp; errors that could lead to a serious incident.&nbsp;&nbsp; Many times, incidents and accidents have been&nbsp; avoided due to cross-checking, which is the common&nbsp;&nbsp; norm in an airliner cockpit - as well as in the&nbsp; cabin with flight attendants and emergency exits.. So with these factors needing to be considered,&nbsp;&nbsp; what sorts of challenges stand in the way of&nbsp; safe and effective single pilot operations? Firstly, the technology simply&nbsp; does not exist at the moment A modern airliner is highly automated. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; the automation and the autoflight system is&nbsp; simply an obedient subordinate of the pilots.&nbsp;&nbsp; It can only do what it is told to do.&nbsp; It does not have a mind of its own. If airliners are to be operated&nbsp; with a single pilot in the cockpit,&nbsp;&nbsp; the automation must have some&nbsp; characteristics that represent&nbsp;&nbsp; the human pilot it is replacing. It must be&nbsp; able to challenge and respond to the pilot,&nbsp;&nbsp; and it must be able to “tell” the pilot what&nbsp; it is doing before the task is completed. The main problem with this is that&nbsp; you can only program a computer&nbsp;&nbsp; to a limit. A human can adapt to the&nbsp; changing environment. Unfortunately,&nbsp;&nbsp; a computer can only perform tasks&nbsp; as per the codes written in it. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; it becomes really difficult to divide the&nbsp; tasks between a human pilot and automation. A simple example is this: let's say an aircraft&nbsp; is on a challenging approach to land and,&nbsp;&nbsp; close to the ground, it starts&nbsp; to rain and the winds pick up.&nbsp;&nbsp; The (Pilot Monitoring) turns on the&nbsp; wiper without asking the (Pilot Flying). The PF does not say anything as he&nbsp; is fully concentrated on flying the&nbsp;&nbsp; aircraft safely onto the runway. After&nbsp; the aircraft is safely on the ground,&nbsp;&nbsp; the PF thanks the PM for turning on the wiper. What happened in this given example is a classic&nbsp; case of human cognitive ability. The PM used&nbsp;&nbsp; his knowledge and understanding of very basic&nbsp; human behavior to make the task easier for his&nbsp;&nbsp; colleague. He knew the PF was not in a position&nbsp; to talk due to the very challenging approach. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; he did what he thought was the best. He turned on&nbsp; the wiper to clear the rain off the windshield. But you might be watching and asking&nbsp; “Well, Couldn’t a computer do it too?” Yes, it could be programmed to detect rain&nbsp; and turn on the wiper. But it first needs to&nbsp;&nbsp; be specifically programmed to respond to&nbsp; a specific amount of rain contamination,&nbsp;&nbsp; for the wipers to be turned on. Sometimes,&nbsp; light rain may not require wipers,&nbsp;&nbsp; and turning them on could distract the&nbsp; pilot. A human pilot, on the other hand,&nbsp;&nbsp; knows with his flying experience how much rain&nbsp; would cause problems for the other pilot and&nbsp;&nbsp; how much would not. A computer cannot realize&nbsp; this, no matter how advanced the technology is. The physical ability of the&nbsp; automated systems is also a question The automation in an aircraft, particularly&nbsp; in a large airliner, is highly integrated and&nbsp;&nbsp; sophisticated. It requires input from various&nbsp; aircraft sensors for it to work properly. If&nbsp;&nbsp; it loses some or all of the data, then it can no&nbsp; longer fly the aircraft. And modern fly-by-wire&nbsp;&nbsp; aircraft become extremely difficult to fly&nbsp; without data inputs to the control system. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; the pilot not only has to perform&nbsp; the non-flying abnormal procedures,&nbsp;&nbsp; but he is also handed over a crippled aircraft&nbsp; that can no longer be flown accurately. In a two-pilot cockpit environment, such a&nbsp; failure becomes easier to manage because one&nbsp;&nbsp; pilot can wholly concentrate on flying the&nbsp; aircraft (the pilot flying) while the other&nbsp;&nbsp; pilot tries to counter the issue with the&nbsp; set procedures. In a single-pilot cockpit,&nbsp;&nbsp; such a failure could easily overwhelm the pilot to&nbsp; the point where he loses his situational awareness&nbsp;&nbsp; and becomes both physically and mentally&nbsp; fatigued. This can lead to a disaster. So, the autopilot systems in&nbsp; an aircraft with single-pilot&nbsp;&nbsp; operations must be more robust&nbsp; and redundant, and more complex. This last point, the complexity, is&nbsp; another interesting talking point.&nbsp;&nbsp; What if the integration, sophistication, and&nbsp; complexity of the automation become so high&nbsp;&nbsp; that a pilot simply fails to understand it? This&nbsp; can get very complicated in automation failure&nbsp;&nbsp; situations where the pilot may have a hard time&nbsp; understanding what has failed and what is working. This can make him mistrust the system to the point&nbsp; where he is not confident in giving certain roles&nbsp;&nbsp; to the automation, again increasing the workload.&nbsp; This can make it extremely hard for the pilot, who&nbsp;&nbsp; has to single-handedly manage all of the systems,&nbsp; the failure, and of course, the flying itself. So, as you can see, there’s still a long way to&nbsp; go before we might see single pilot operations. And while we’re pretty sure&nbsp; we know how most of you would&nbsp;&nbsp; feel about the prospect of having&nbsp; only one pilot flying an aircraft,&nbsp;&nbsp; we’d love to hear what you think about it&nbsp; all - and the reasons for your position. Leave a comment and let us know! Thanks for watching!

called your private pilot's license or ppl for short this license is the starting point of your aviation journey and is where you will learn all of the essentials required to operate an aircraft safely under your own authority once you've completed your private pilot's license you can then continue your training to attain other licenses and ratings such as the multi-engine rating or the ifr instrument rating and even the commercial pilot's license which allows you to fly for higher as a professional working pilot but you must first attain your private pilot's license now the prerequisites for the private pilot's license are as follows you must be at least 17 years of age in order to hold the license however you can start your training at a slightly younger age you must also pass a category three medical exam obtained from a certified aviation medical examiner and although not required i also recommend going on what's called a demo flight or an introductory flight where you get to go with a flight instructor and kind of fly around for a little bit to take you on a short flight you can kind of test the waters to see if flight training is something that you really want to do the timeline required to complete your private license ranges anywhere from one to six months depending on how frequently you fly and how quickly you are progressing if you fly three times a week you're likely to finish much sooner than someone who only flies say for instance once a week for example it took me five months to complete my private license but i was under no immediate time or strength and i flew approximately once twice a week now there are two primary components the private pilots license that must be completed in past the written exam and the practical flight test ground school whether done in class or as an online platform is where you will learn everything from aircraft systems weather and navigation to general information as well as air law ground school prepares you with all the information required to complete and pass your written exam when i started my private pilot's license ground school consisted of two classes per week with each class running approximately three hours in length at first it was a lot of new information in a short period of time especially for a new pilot so i found it useful to review the material after each and every class and in any spare time i had to make sure i retained as much of the information as possible i would even recommend supplementing your ground school training with other online courses or books to assist in furthering your understanding of the material your flight school will have a specific curriculum to follow and it should cover all the topics required for the written exam then the fun started actually learning to fly just like the ground school portion there is a curriculum to follow for the private pilot's license practical training as part of your private license training there are minimum flight hour requirements that must be met you must complete a total of 40 hours total flight time before you can take the practical flight test of these 40 hours 20 hours must be dual instruction time with your flight instructor which must include the following three hours of cross-country flight three hours of night flight instruction three hours of instrument flying relying on your aircraft instruments only and three hours of flight test preparation you must also complete a minimum of 10 hours of solo flying and that means exactly how it sounds flying by yourself for a minimum of 10 hours of which five hours must be cross country time keep in mind that these are the absolute minimum flight hours required in order to qualify for the flight tests it is up to your instructor and when they feel comfortable to determine how well you are progressing through your training and when they think you are ready for the test it is likely you will need more than 40 hours of flight time before you are even ready and confident enough looking at my logbook i had approximately 60 flight hours actually closer to 70 hours logged before i took my flight test there are always components of your training that will require you to dedicate additional time for me it was landings i spent an additional 10 hours actually even close to 15 hours of flight time just practicing my landings probably an additional five hours of upper air work and a few hours here and there practicing other parts of my flying before i felt confident enough for the test as part of your flight training budget don't forget to consider this add a little bit extra in there for the additional training i don't think there's anyone out there who can confidently say they aced every single part of their flight training without having to put in a little extra time and a little bit of extra practice here and there also i should mention that different countries have different minimum flight training requirements canada for instance is slightly different than the us so make sure you consult your flight school on what the minimums are for your specific training now your flight school should have a specific curriculum to follow for the in-flight training just like the ground school each flight starts with a pre-flight briefing where you and your instructor sit down and discuss what the plan is for that particular lesson and ends with a debrief after the flight is completed your first few flights will be focused primarily around basic handling and maneuvering of the aircraft my first two lessons involved learning the basics of straight level flight climbs descents as well as turns coordinated flight and really just getting comfortable at the controls after that the next few lessons focus on learning upper air works such as slow flight stalls steep turns and number of other maneuvers in different aircraft configurations like monkey see monkey do my instructor would demonstrate a particular maneuver and then i would perform it under their watchful eye your instructor will coach you throughout and don't be discouraged if some maneuvers take several attempts usually on the flight back to the airport your instructor will likely quiz you on in-flight emergencies and and have you perform some basic navigation using the maps there are always things to be learning and practicing even when just flying to and from the airport in the practice area once your instructor feels confident in your ability to control the aircraft in the different phases of flight throughout the first few lessons that's when they start teaching you how to land for me learning how to land started on my sixth flight lesson but like i said this is dependent on your confidence in the aircraft and how comfortable your instructor is with your flying progression just like the lessons before my instructor would first demonstrate a perfect landing and then give me control of the airplane and talk me through the process after talking me through a few more landings i was able to get at least a good grasp as far as how to do a normal landing without too much difficulty of course there was always a lot of room for improvement but the subsequent lessons would offer me ample time for practice for the next few flights after my first few landings we would spend all of our time in what's called the circuit practicing takeoffs and landings and doing touch and goes the circuit is essentially a pattern flown in the vicinity of the airport and it allows you to practice your takeoffs approaches and landings several times in a shorter period of time it also helps you get very comfortable with your checklist as you are doing them repeatedly every time you fly once around the pattern in fact here's my actual log book showing just how many times i actually went up and practiced takeoffs and landings in the circuit with my instructor by now you've actually come a long ways you can safely take off perform all the basic maneuvers communicate a little bit with air traffic control navigate around the local area and land the airplane without too much difficulty however one of the most notable times around all of your private pilot's license training is about to happen your first solo ask any pilot today what they remember from their flight training and i guarantee you they won't be able to remember many specific days of their flight training but they will for sure be able to recall almost every detail about their first solo flight like i said your takeoffs and landings are feeling pretty good by now but you are about to fly the plane for the first time completely by yourself from takeoff to landing and everything in between while you're in the circuit this doesn't mean you have your license yet however but it is the first time that you will be completely alone in the airplane nobody beside you to take control if things get a little bit hairy and it is a true sign that your instructor has confidence in your flying abilities to that point it is also a critical part in building your confidence for the rest of your training my first solo started with my instructor and i first practicing a few takeoffs and landings just to get in the swing of things before she told me to taxi to the ramp area where she could get out from the ramp area she stood with a two-way radio to watch me for the first in my life taxi the plane take off fly a circuit and land completely by myself although my first landing flying solo wasn't the prettiest and actually resulted in me having to circle around again and attempt a second landing i tell you what there is nothing more gratifying than when you accomplish your first solo flight upon completing your first solo you've reached an important milestone and at this point training becomes even more interesting after i completed my first solo subsequent lessons consisted of more advanced skills such as navigation shortened soft field takeoffs and landings basic instrument flying some night flying among other things as part of the curriculum and even a little bit more advanced communications and airport operations we also practiced diversions forced landings in the event of an engine failure and several other emergency procedures in between the dual lessons with my instructor i would also fly solo in order to practice the lessons that i had already learned this of course after already had proven that i could operate the airplane safely by myself having completed my first solo being able to fly solo in combination with my dual training was also really beneficial because i could take the things i was learning with my instructor and apply them to my solo flying the next part of my training was cross-country flying because i needed my cross-country flying hours completing the cross-country portion of the training was when i found that all my training was starting to come together cohesively up to that point i was always flying to and from the same airport to and from the practice area or flying in the circuit practicing takeoffs and landings cross-country training teaches you to fly to different airports and introduces you to new flying environments every airport offers its new set of challenges add weather navigation different air space different air traffic control and everything else that you've learned so far i personally found this to be one of my favorite parts of the train because i was now applying my flying skills to actually fly somewhere different in order to be efficient with the training my instructor and i would fly triangle cross-country flights where we would stop at two other airports along the way this gave me more opportunity to practice my landings and expose me to some other airport environments the next cross country i flew the exact same trip but this time did it solo a few more cross-country trips with my instructor after that and a few more trips solar provided me with a lot of practice and not to mention it also contributed to my solo and solo cross country hour requirements keep in mind there's also a solo cross country that is required that must be at least 150 nautical miles you can do a stop in between as long as those stops are at least 50 nautical miles the instrument portion of the training was next and involved practicing flying using your aircraft instruments only this basically trains you to maintain control of the aircraft in the event that you accidentally fly into cloud and lose visual reference to the horizon my instructor simulated several different scenarios where you might enter into cloud accidentally and i had to practice maintaining control of the aircraft while wearing a pair of what's called foggles basically glasses that block out your view of the outside horizon to simulate flying and cloud we practiced the instrument flying over a couple of flights out to the practice area and while we were in route on our cross-country flights a few hours of training using just your instruments gives you a small taste of the much more advanced training required in ifr flying but it's just that a taste next was the night training portion in completing my night hours my instructor had me fly a triangle cross country again where we practice night landings at different airports and practice night navigation this of course had to be done during night hours in more northern latitudes the sun sets much later during the summer and much earlier in the winter i was lucky enough to be doing my night hours during the winter otherwise i may have had to wait till 10 o'clock p.m before the sun would set before i could start my training to this date night time is still perhaps one of my favorite times to fly the visibility is usually great you can see cities roads and the view is spectacular of the stars once you've reached this point it's time to review everything that you've learned the only thing standing in between you and your license now is the practical flight test in order to meet the three-hour flight test preparation requirement hours i went back through my training log with my instructor and she highlighted things that we could go review together before she would recommend me for the test now there are two portions to the practical flight test the oral and the in-flight portions the oral portion is done on the ground with the examiner before your flight where they quiz you on everything from weather aircraft performance and navigation to the flight plan details and everything in between pertinent to that day's flight and conditions once that is completed you then get in the airplane and perform the practical portion of the test this involves completing all of the flight maneuvers within certain tolerances of altitude airspeed and performance as well as proving your competence in cross-country flying navigation communication and aircraft handling they will simulate some emergencies in flight and grade you based on your performance although this may sound daunting right now remember this is after all of your training is completed so you'll have practice and be confident in everything you need to know when i did my flight test i remember feeling a lot more nervous than i probably looked but remember you're basically just performing the things that you have already practiced many times over so don't let the nervousness diminish your confidence also you are human i remember my examiner was more impressed if i knew where and how to find the information i didn't know rather than just trying to remember everything my flight test took a little under two hours of flight time and the ground portion was maybe over an hour before i knew it i had passed the exam and was back on the ground and officially a private pilot after signing some paperwork with the instructor and the examiner they gave me a temporary license to hold me over until my official license arrived in the mail it looks like this kind of like a passport but a heck of a lot cooler if you with the GPS all of these things that used to completely overwhelm you this low pressure flight can remind you of why you're putting in all of this hard work sometimes a casual pleasure flight is the best thing to do to reawaken your excitement for flying so if you're starting to get burned out give yourself permission to go up and have some fun I mean we all started flying because we thought it was fun in the first place also I want you to consider connecting with the others in the aviation Community talking to other Pilots especially those that remember their days as students this can give you perspective and moral support you'll find that everyone has their War stories about training maybe it's the tale of taking three times to Solo or nearly giving up an instrument training hearing these stories from others can make you feel less isolated you could join a student pilot Facebook group or just chat with other Pilots at your school there's a real comfort in hearing oh yeah I did that too here's how I got past it it reminds you that struggling is part of the process and that you not failing I want to talk about your mindset because the mental game is huge in Flight Training right now you may be flooded with self-doubt thoughts like I'll never get this I'm a terrible pilot or my instructor might think I'm helpless these thoughts might be swirling in your head I've been there and it's not a fun place we often are our worst critic so how do we shift our mindset first you need to be kind to yourself realize that learning to fly is challenging for everyone struggling doesn't mean you're stupid or incapable it means that you're learning try to reframe mistakes as lessons rather than failures every time you mess up you're discovering one way of how not to do it and that means that you are one step closer to figuring out how to do it instead of saying I suck at steep turns just say I haven't gotten it quite yet that one little word yet is a very very powerful word it keeps the door open for improvement and reminds you this is a journey second you need to stop the unhealthy comparison this is your flight training not anybody else's it may take you 50 hours to solo well your buddy only took 20 so what flight training is not a race in fact in fact the national average for getting your private pilot's license is somewhere between 65 and 70 hours most Pilots take more than the 40 hour minimum to get their license I've seen some students take 45 hours I've seen some take 80 or 100 and guess what in the end they all earn the same certificate no one at the airlines or the local pilot Club is going to care how many hours it took for you to get your license if anything I would say pilots who train at a slower steadier Pace often end up more solid in their skills than the pilots that crammed through it rushing through it in minimum time isn't a badge of honor it can actually leave huge gaps in your experience embrace the pace the goal here is to make you a safe and competent pilot not a low time pilot third you need to focus on small wins while you're in a slump it's so easy to fixate on everything that you're doing wrong instead I want you to actively try to look for things that you're doing better this time than you did last time maybe your radio calls were better this week maybe you finally nailed the crosswing correction on takeoff even if the rest of the flight was rough celebrate those small victories maybe even write them down when I was a student I used to have a little notebook that I'd keep in my flight bag where after each flight I jot down what I thought I did well on that flight over time I would be able to look back at the did well list and it would be a huge confident boost it's also helpful to remember that success isn't measured in Maneuvers mastered sometimes it could be confidence improved or fear overcome did you feel a little less nervous in the plane today than he did a month ago that counts as progress finally I want you to visualize where you're headed it might sound a bit fluffy but spend a minute picturing yourself as a fully certified pilot imagine how proud that you is with the fact that you didn't give up sure yourself taking your friends up flying on a sunny weekend day or landing at that bucket list airport holding that Vision close to you is going to help you pull through on those hard days because you know what you're working towards every frustrating lesson is a step towards you becoming that confident pilot that you're picturing now that we've talked about why and how to shift your mind Mind Set Let's get into some more practical tips these are all things that I've picked up from my personal experience as well as instructing tons of other students you could try one of them you could try all of them the idea is to shake things up and try to tackle this slump head on number one I want you to talk to your instructor openly have an honest heart-to-heart conversation with your CFI about how you're feeling and why you're feeling this way good instructors care about your progress and your morale not just the syllabus let them know hey man I've been feeling pretty discouraged with my Landings lately or whatever the issue may be a great instructor is going to work with you to readjust your game plan for instance they might take a step back and have you work on the fundamentals for a little bit or they might just try explaining things in a different way also ask for specific feedback during your debrief request concrete pointers on what to improve on if the debriefs feel like they've been too short or too vague speak up and ask them to be a little bit more thorough with you I mean that's what you're paying them for right sometimes just having a clear action item like bringing your eyes up to the end of the runway when you're flaring or maybe bringing your flaps down just a second earlier can give you a sense of direction and control it's going to ease the frustration remember your instructor is on your team they've probably seen a ton of students struggle with exactly the same things that you are struggling with right now together you two can come up with a plan to fix it number two is break it down into bite-sized goals when you're stuck on a big skill break it into smaller pieces this is a classic technique if Landing the plane feels overwhelming each lap focus on one specific aspect of the pattern maybe one lap focus on your approach speed one lap practice leveling off at your altitude by isolating those subskills you can start to see improvements in those small areas this is going to eventually add up to you mastering the entire thing tell your instructor that you'd like to practice in a stepbystep way for example if steep turns are giving you trouble first practice holding altitude and gentle turns then gradually increase the bank as you get more and more comfortable build into it each little Victory builds confidence for the next step slow and steady wins the race don't try to tackle the entire maneuver perfectly the first time break it down step by step number three is change the scenery or routine Sometimes the best way to jolt yourself out of a rut is to do something different our brains love novelty if you've been grinding away at the same airport every time ask your instructor if you guys can fly over to a different airport for this flight a simple change in scenery can make flying fun again this might even also present a different learning opportunity new runways different airspace Etc if radio communications are stressing you out maybe try a lesson at an unted airport to usually train bright and early in the morning maybe try an afternoon flight the point is to break the monotony and remind yourself that flying isn't just repeating one maneuver or one thing over and over and over again it's about experiencing new things I've taken students that were sick of touch and goes out on a cross country and the result is when they get back to the pattern they feel refreshed they're often performing better because their mindset has shifted away way onto something else so don't hesitate to tell your instructor that you need to change it up it's absolutely a valid part to do a fun flight or just change it up in any way tip number four is fly with a different instructor this one can be intimidating but it works wonder if you're hitting a plateau consider flying with a different instructor for a lesson or two each instructor has their own style and perspective on things a different instructor might explain something just a tiny bit different and that might be enough to make it click in your brain this isn't about replacing your current instructor unless you think that they're just overall a good fit but that's a different topic it's about getting a fresh set of eyes on your flying as an instructor myself I encourage my students to fly with other instructors all the time I don't take it personally at all in fact good instructors know that they probably can't cover everything perfectly with every student and often you want those other perspectives from other instructors you might feel a little bit nervous when you're flying with somebody new that's totally normal but often that little shakeup in your routine is what forces you to grow I've seen students have breakthrough throughs on flights with other instructors or students who belong to other instructors come and fly with me all because I said something just a tiny bit different even the act of adapting to a slightly different communication style in the airplane is going to make you a more versatile pilot so if you're comfortable with it give it a try you focus it on like hey Mr instructor I'd like to go fly with this other instructor just for a lesson to get a different perspective on my Landings is that okay with you nine times out of 10 they're going to support it the goal here is your progress so whatever helps helps tip number five and you need to be careful with this one but it's take a short break if you need it I'm not giving you an excuse to procrastinate indefinitely sometimes a brief step away can reset your mindset if you've been flying super frequently and it's starting to turn into a source of dread consider taking a week or two offline use that time to rest and recharge and study sometimes a little mental break can help your brain consolidate skills when you come back you might just find out that things make more sense for example if you've flown for 5 days in a row and you feels like it's getting worse each day take the weekend off don't even look at airplanes for a couple days don't even think about airplanes for a couple of days after that you're going to come back fresh often the next lesson after a short keyword short break often ends up being surprisingly good this is because you're not as mentally fatigued the caveat here is to you can't let this short break turn into you quitting Mark a datea on your calendar that you're going to get back into it during your break you can still stay engaged at home by chair flying watching some inspirational Aviation videos or reading about flying keep the passion alive without the performance pressure for a little bit this is just a strategic pause it's not a full stop tip number six reevaluate your schedule and habit sometimes external pressures can amplify your feeling of being stuck are you flying often enough to retain what you've been learning or your lessons too far apart like let's say one flight every 3 to four week you might feel like one step forward two steps back each time if possible try to fly more often even if that means slightly shorter flights or finding more money in your budget consistency really really helps momentum on the flip side if you're flying every day and then studying till midnight you might burn yourself out balance is the key here also check in on stress outside of flying is school going okay is work going okay is stuff at home okay this stuff can absolutely bleed into your performance in the airplane if life stress is high you need to acknowledge that it's harder to focus and be kind to yourself maybe try to schedule your lessons at a time of day where you're the most alert or do a quick breathing exercise in your car before you go into the airport to kind of reset yourself basically you need to set yourself up for Success before each flight by managing the things outside of the airplane I want to close here with some encouragement to you this tough phase that you are in it will pass I know it often feels endless when you're in the middle of it but one day maybe sooner than you think you're going to look back on it and think about how far you've come next flight where things really start to click is right around the corner progress often feels invisible until it isn't so suddenly you'll have the Breakthrough moment where it all comes together and you'll wonder why you've ever doubted yourself in the first place remember every pilot that you admire has been in your shoes and been where you are before they felt the frustration the self-doubt the temptation to quit but they kept going and that's the reason why they're Pilots now you are on the same hero's journey the obstacles that you are facing now are shaping you into becoming a safer better pilot it's teaching you patience resilience and problem solving and all of these are qualities that you want in a pilot and when you do over come this hurdle you can carry the confidence of it with you forever so keep showing up and keep chipping away at those learning plateaus knowing that you will overcome them if one strategy doesn't work try another lean on your love for flying let it pull you through the hard days and remember whenever that inner critic tries to bring you down remember where you started and where you're at now one day in the not too distant future you'll be the one telling the next generation of student Pilots that you remember being in their shoes stick through it and I promise you it is all worth it if you enjoyed this video do me a favor hit the like button and consider subscribing and I'll see you next week

- Documentary #02 : Pilots Memorize This Number Before Every Flight? || #aviation  #safety  #airlines: Kind: captions Language: en Welcome back, champions of the skies and seekers of the unseen forces that shape flight. Your support powers this journey into the heart of aviation secrets. And today, we're unraveling a mystery that separates mere flying machines from aerodynamic legends. The best liftto drag ratio. You may have heard pilots whisper about glide distances or engineers obsess over efficiency numbers. But what really is this liftto drag ratio? And why does it matter so much that it could mean the difference between soaring glory and a deadly fall? Imagine this, an aircraft slicing through the sky. It battles two invisible forces. Lift, the force that holds it up, and drag, the relentless resistance trying to slow it down. The lift to drag ratio measures just how well that aircraft fights to stay aloft without being dragged back. The higher the number, the more glide distance you get for every drop in altitude. Simple yet critical. Now, here's the twist. The best liftto drag ratio is the point where the aircraft is at its most efficient. Generating the most lift for the least drag. It's the sweet spot of fragile balance. And hitting it means your plane can glide farther, fly smoother, and save precious fuel. So, who nails this balance perfectly? Enter the world of gliders. Those sleek, silent hunters of the sky. Some gliders boast LD ratios exceeding 60, letting them glide for miles without an engine. Picture soaring like a bird for hours on just the faintest whisper of rising air. Pure aerodynamic poetry. But what about the mighty fighter jets? The legendary F-22 Raptor, a stealthy king of the skies, has a best LD ratio of about 9 to 10. Much lower than gliders, but impressive given its speed and agility demands. Fighters sacrifice pure glide efficiency for unmatched maneuverability and power, trading off lift to drag for dominance in battle. Commercial airliners fall somewhere in between with ratios around 15 to 20, designed to cruise efficiently over thousands of miles, balancing passenger comfort and fuel economy. Here's why this matters. When engines fail or fuel runs low, knowing your aircraft's best liftto drag ratio could be the difference between a safe glide to a runway or a fatal plunge. For pilots, it's a secret weapon, a lifesaving number etched deep into their training. So next time you look up at a jet screaming through the clouds, remember behind its roar lies a hidden dance of forces. And the best liftto drag ratio is the silent hero keeping it aloft. Keep your eyes on the skies, heroes, because understanding the invisible forces of flight brings us closer to mastering the ultimate frontier, the freedom of the air.
